chapters.chapter: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

topics.meaning: A model is a simplified way of representing a real system, focusing only on the details that matter for a given question.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The natural world is complex, and studying it in full detail is often impossible, so science uses models that are simplified ways of looking at real systems and focus only on what is most important for a given question. In physics a moving car may be represented as a single point, in chemistry atoms and molecules are drawn as spheres and bonds, in biology cells are shown as diagrams highlighting key parts, and in earth science the Earth may be treated as a smooth layered sphere. Building such models involves making assumptions and deliberately ignoring certain details, such as neglecting air resistance when studying a falling object or ignoring individual cells when studying how the heart pumps blood. These choices are not mistakes but are made on purpose to keep things simple enough while still allowing us to find answers. As models become more complex, we add extra details for greater accuracy.
topics.easyExplanation: A model keeps the important details and ignores the unimportant ones so a hard problem becomes easy to think about.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What a model is
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A model is a simplified picture of a real system that keeps only the parts useful for a particular question.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make assumptions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We decide which details matter, like the mass and speed of a cricket ball, and which can be ignored, like its colour.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Ignore on purpose
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We deliberately leave out small effects such as air resistance or seam stitching to keep the model simple.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add detail when needed
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If we need more accuracy, we put back some of the ignored details to build a more complex model.

examples: A cricket ball hit for a six is modelled using its mass, speed and direction, while its colour and the brand of the bat are ignored.
When studying how the heart pumps blood, the organ is treated as a working system and individual cells are ignored.

importantKeywords.definition: A simplified representation of a real system that focuses on the most important features.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Showing a moving car as a single point.

importantKeywords.definition: A condition we accept as true to simplify a model, even if it is not exactly correct.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Assuming there is no air resistance on a falling ball.

importantKeywords.definition: The part of the world we choose to study, separated from its surroundings.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The heart treated as a blood-pumping system.

topics.quickSummary: Science uses models, which are simplified versions of real systems that keep only the details important for a given question. Making a model means choosing useful details and deliberately ignoring others, such as ignoring air resistance for a falling object. These simplifications are intentional, and more detail can be added later for greater accuracy. Models let us answer questions about a complex world.
revisionNotes: Models simplify complex systems for a chosen question.
Useful details are kept; unimportant ones are ignored on purpose.
Different subjects model the same world in different ways.
Adding back detail makes a model more accurate but more complex.

topics.meaning: Science gives everyday words very specific meanings and uses shared symbols and units so ideas can be communicated clearly worldwide.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: As you explore science more deeply, you notice that it uses language in a very careful and precise way, because many everyday words such as force, work, cell, or reaction have specific scientific meanings. These meanings must be exact so that scientific ideas are communicated clearly and unambiguously. To allow scientists across the world to describe observations, compare results, and build ideas together, science uses a shared language of specific terms, symbols, and units. Quantities such as mass, velocity, force, and electric current are represented by symbols like m, v, F, and I, each associated with a defined unit. Standard international units, such as the kilogram, allow results to be compared and ensure fairness in trade and daily life. The symbol c for the speed of light, for example, comes from the Latin word celeritas meaning speed.
topics.easyExplanation: Science uses exact words, symbols and agreed units so everyone everywhere understands the same thing.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Everyday words get exact meaning
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Words like force and work mean something very specific in science, unlike in casual speech.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use symbols for quantities
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Mass, velocity, force and current are written as m, v, F and I to save space and avoid confusion.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Attach a defined unit
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Every quantity carries an agreed unit, such as the kilogram for mass.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use standard international units
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Standard SI units let scientists everywhere compare results and avoid errors from mismatched units.

examples: The speed of light is denoted c, from the Latin celeritas meaning speed, and is defined as exactly 299792458 m/s.
An aircraft once ran out of fuel because the crew used pounds per litre instead of kilograms per litre, showing why standard units matter.

importantKeywords.definition: A letter or sign used to represent a physical quantity.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Using m for mass and F for force.

importantKeywords.definition: An agreed standard amount used to measure a quantity.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The kilogram used to measure mass.

importantKeywords.definition: The standard International System of units used by scientists worldwide.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Using metres and kilograms instead of local measures.

topics.quickSummary: Science uses a careful and precise language in which everyday words gain exact meanings, and quantities are written using agreed symbols such as m, v, F and I. Each quantity has a defined unit, and standard SI units allow results to be compared fairly across the world. Using mismatched units can cause serious errors, as in the aircraft fuel incident. This shared language lets scientists communicate clearly.
revisionNotes: Scientific words have precise, fixed meanings.
Quantities are written using symbols like m, v, F, I.
Each quantity has a defined unit.
Standard SI units prevent confusion and errors.

topics.topic: Mathematics as the Language of Science
topics.meaning: Mathematics in science is a language for expressing relationships between quantities clearly, not just a tool for calculating numbers.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Science often turns to mathematics so that relationships between quantities can be expressed clearly and tested carefully. An equation is not just a calculation tool, it is a compact statement about how certain things are related, such as how distance, time, and velocity connect to tell us where an object will be later. Mathematical expressions are also used to describe rates of chemical reactions, patterns of population growth, or changes in energy within a system. Learning to use mathematics in science does not mean memorising equations; it means understanding the situation first, identifying the relevant quantities, and then using mathematical relationships to reason carefully. In this way mathematics becomes a powerful language for thinking, not just for finding numerical answers. If you focus first on understanding the situation, equations begin to feel less like obstacles and more like helpful guides.
topics.easyExplanation: Equations are short sentences that tell us how different quantities are connected, helping us reason about the world.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand the situation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Begin by picturing what is physically happening before reaching for any formula.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify relevant quantities
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Decide which measurable things, like distance, time or energy, actually matter.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Express the relationship
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write an equation that compactly states how those quantities are connected.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reason, do not just memorise
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use the relationship to think and predict, rather than memorising it blindly.

examples: Using distance, time and velocity together lets us predict where a moving object will be at a later moment.
Mathematical expressions describe rates of chemical reactions, population growth, or changes in energy in a system.

importantKeywords.definition: A compact statement showing how quantities are related.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Linking distance, time and velocity to find a position.

importantKeywords.definition: Something that can be measured and given a value and unit.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Time measured in seconds.

importantKeywords.definition: A connection between quantities expressed mathematically.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Velocity equals distance divided by time.

topics.quickSummary: In science, mathematics is a language for clearly expressing and testing how quantities are related, not merely a way to crunch numbers. An equation is a compact statement of a relationship, useful for describing motion, reactions, populations and energy. The key skill is to understand the situation, identify the quantities, and reason with the relationship rather than memorise it. Used this way, equations become guides for thinking.
revisionNotes: Mathematics expresses relationships between quantities.
An equation is a compact statement, not just a calculation.
Understand the situation before using a formula.
Reason with equations instead of memorising them.

topics.meaning: In science, laws describe regular patterns in nature, theories explain why those patterns occur, and principles are broad ideas that guide understanding.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: As observations are repeated, measurements refined, and ideas tested, we organise our understanding using terms that have specific scientific meanings. A law usually describes a regular pattern observed in nature, often expressed in words or mathematical relationships, such as Newton's laws of motion explaining the jerk felt when a bus stops suddenly. A theory goes a step further and provides an explanation of why those patterns occur, based on evidence gathered over time, such as the atomic theory explaining how molecules form. Principles are broad ideas that help us make sense of a situation, such as the principle of conservation of energy applied when climbing stairs. Importantly, in science a theory does not mean a guess or untested idea; it is an explanation based on careful testing and critical examination, and it remains open to improvement as new evidence appears.
topics.easyExplanation: A law tells us what pattern happens, a theory explains why it happens, and a principle is a big idea that guides us.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Law describes a pattern
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A law states a regular pattern seen in nature, like Newton's laws of motion.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Theory explains the pattern
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A theory gives an evidence-based explanation of why the pattern occurs, like atomic theory.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Principle gives broad guidance
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A principle is a wide idea, like conservation of energy, used across many situations.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Theories can change
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Scientific theories are well tested but stay open to revision when new evidence appears.

examples: Newton's laws of motion explain the jerk felt when a bus stops suddenly.
The atomic theory explains how molecules are formed, and the principle of conservation of energy applies when climbing stairs.

importantKeywords.definition: A statement describing a regular pattern observed in nature.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Newton's laws describing how objects move.

importantKeywords.definition: A well-tested, evidence-based explanation of why patterns occur.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Atomic theory explaining how molecules form.

importantKeywords.definition: A broad idea used to make sense of many situations.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Conservation of energy applied to climbing stairs.

topics.quickSummary: Science organises knowledge using laws, theories and principles. A law describes a regular pattern, a theory explains why the pattern happens based on evidence, and a principle is a broad guiding idea. A scientific theory is not a guess but a carefully tested explanation that stays open to revision. This openness to correction by evidence is what makes science reliable.
revisionNotes: A law describes a regular natural pattern.
A theory explains why a pattern occurs, based on evidence.
A principle is a broad, widely applicable idea.
A scientific theory is well tested, not a mere guess.

topics.meaning: Prediction is the use of well-established laws, theories and models to anticipate what will happen under new conditions, based on evidence and reasoning.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: One of the most remarkable strengths of science is its ability to make predictions, because when laws, theories, and models are well established they let us anticipate what will happen under new or different conditions, often even before or without an experiment. Using ideas about motion we can predict how far a kicked football will travel, using chemical knowledge we can estimate how much carbon dioxide will be produced, and using biological principles we can predict how breathing changes while running. These predictions are not guesses but reasoned expectations based on evidence and careful thinking. When predictions match observations, confidence in the underlying science grows, but when they do not, scientists re-examine their assumptions, models, or measurements. A good prediction should be scientifically testable, asking for measurable evidence and past patterns rather than relying on vague impressions.
topics.easyExplanation: Science uses tested ideas to make reasoned guesses about what will happen, and checks them against real observations.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Start from tested ideas
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use well-established laws, theories or models as the basis for the prediction.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reason out the expectation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Apply those ideas carefully to a new situation to anticipate the result.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make it testable
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Frame the prediction so it can be checked with measurable evidence, not vague impressions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compare and revise
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If observations disagree, re-examine the assumptions, model or measurements.

examples: Using ideas about motion, we can predict how far a kicked football will travel.
To test a claim that it will rain, one asks measurable questions about humidity, wind speed and temperature rather than just whether clouds look dark.

importantKeywords.definition: A reasoned expectation of what will happen, based on evidence and tested ideas.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Estimating how much carbon dioxide a reaction will produce.

importantKeywords.definition: Able to be checked using measurable evidence.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Measuring humidity to test a rain forecast.

importantKeywords.definition: Measurable data and observed patterns used to support reasoning.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Past humidity readings before it rained.

topics.quickSummary: A major strength of science is prediction, where well-established laws, theories and models let us anticipate outcomes in new situations. Predictions are reasoned expectations grounded in evidence, not guesses, and good ones are testable using measurable data. When predictions match observations, confidence grows; when they fail, scientists revisit their assumptions. Prediction therefore drives deeper exploration and understanding.
revisionNotes: Prediction anticipates outcomes using tested ideas.
Predictions are reasoned, not guesses.
Good predictions are testable with measurable evidence.
Failed predictions lead scientists to revise their ideas.

topics.meaning: Even successful scientific theories have limits and may fail under new conditions, and being correctable by evidence is a key strength of science.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Even the most successful scientific theories have limits and may fail when new conditions are explored or when measurements become more precise. Such failures are not a weakness of science; in fact they are its greatest strength, because when predictions do not match observations, scientists do not reject ideas based on opinion or belief but only on evidence. No scientific theory is ever final and none is beyond question, and this openness to being corrected by nature itself is what has allowed science to help us understand the world. This same scientific thinking helps evaluate viral claims, such as the false idea that food becomes harmful during an eclipse, by asking whether any real physical, chemical, or biological mechanism supports the claim. An eclipse is just a play of shadows, with no significant temperature change, so no mechanism makes food unsafe.
topics.easyExplanation: No scientific idea is final; science improves whenever evidence shows an idea is wrong, and that is its strength.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Theories have limits
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Every theory works only within certain conditions and may fail beyond them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Failure means progress
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When a prediction fails, it reveals where our understanding must improve.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Judge by evidence
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Ideas are accepted or rejected based on evidence, not on opinion or belief.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Question viral claims
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use scientific thinking to ask whether a real mechanism supports a popular claim.

examples: A theory may fail when measurements become more precise or new conditions are explored.
The claim that food becomes harmful during an eclipse fails because an eclipse is only a play of shadows with no mechanism to spoil food.

importantKeywords.definition: The boundary of conditions within which a theory works.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A theory failing when measurements get very precise.

importantKeywords.definition: A physical, chemical or biological process that could cause an effect.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Asking what process could make food unsafe during an eclipse.

importantKeywords.definition: The way science revises ideas when evidence contradicts them.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Revising a model after a failed prediction.

topics.quickSummary: Even the best scientific theories have limits and can fail under new or more precise conditions, but this is a strength because science corrects itself using evidence rather than opinion. No theory is final or beyond question, and openness to correction is what makes science reliable. The same critical thinking lets us test viral claims by asking whether any real mechanism supports them, as with the false eclipse-and-food belief.
revisionNotes: All theories have limits and can fail.
Failures drive science to improve.
Ideas are judged only by evidence.
Critical thinking exposes false viral claims.

topics.meaning: Estimation is making a rough but reasonable calculation to check whether an answer makes sense, valuing careful reasoning over exact numbers.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: As you continue through science, a helpful strategy is to first understand the situation, then identify the quantities that matter, and finally make a rough estimate to check whether an answer makes sense. Exact values are not always necessary, especially in the early stages of reasoning, and often an approximate estimate is enough to tell us whether a result is reasonable or impossible. Learning to estimate helps you build intuition, detect errors, and develop confidence in your thinking, and science values careful reasoning perhaps even more than accurate calculations. For example, to estimate the air breathed in a day, one assumes about 12 to 15 breaths per minute over 1440 minutes, giving roughly 20 thousand breaths, and with about half a litre per breath this gives roughly 10000 litres a day. The aim is not an exact number but to judge whether the answer is sensible.
topics.easyExplanation: Estimation is a quick rough calculation that checks if an answer is sensible, which matters more than getting an exact figure.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand the situation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: First be clear about what is being asked and what is happening.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pick the key quantities
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Identify the few numbers, like breaths per minute, that drive the answer.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make a rough calculation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Combine the quantities to get an approximate value.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check if it is sensible
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Ask whether the result is reasonable, since a wildly large or small answer signals an error.

examples: We breathe roughly 20 thousand breaths a day at about 0.5 litre each, giving an estimate of about 10000 litres of air per day.
Estimating the rice a family of four needs for a month, assuming all calories come from rice, checks whether 100 g for a month is too little.

importantKeywords.definition: A rough calculation used to judge whether an answer is reasonable.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Guessing about 10000 litres of air breathed per day.

importantKeywords.definition: A value that is close enough rather than exact.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Saying roughly 20 thousand breaths a day.

importantKeywords.definition: A result that makes sense given the situation.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: An estimate that is neither far too small nor far too large.

topics.quickSummary: Estimation means understanding a situation, identifying the quantities that matter, and making a rough calculation to check whether an answer makes sense. Exact values are not always needed, and an approximate estimate often reveals whether a result is reasonable or impossible. Estimation builds intuition and helps detect errors, and science values careful reasoning even more than precise calculation.
revisionNotes: Estimation gives a rough but useful answer.
Identify key quantities before estimating.
Estimates check whether a result is sensible.
Careful reasoning matters more than exact numbers.

topics.topic: Science as a Connected Human Activity
topics.meaning: Science is a creative, collaborative human activity whose branches are connected to one another and to other ways of knowing the world.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: After Grade 10 science divides into branches like physics, chemistry, biology and earth science, but the natural world does not have such boundaries; these divisions are made only to help organise knowledge and are not independent of each other. Most real-world problems today, such as understanding climate change, developing medicines, or designing sustainable technologies, require ideas from several disciplines together, and science also connects naturally with mathematics, technology, arts, and social sciences. Understanding even a simple mask requires physics, chemistry, biology and mathematics together. Science is not just a collection of facts, equations, or experiments but a human activity shaped by curiosity, creativity, collaboration, and careful questioning, growing as people ask questions, test ideas, share results, and learn from mistakes across cultures and generations. Scientific thinking helps everyone understand technology and evaluate information critically.
topics.easyExplanation: Science is one connected human effort; its branches overlap and work with maths, arts and society to solve real problems.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Branches are not separate
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Physics, chemistry, biology and earth science are divisions made for convenience, not real boundaries.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Real problems need many fields
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Issues like climate change or new medicines draw on several disciplines at once.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Science links with other knowledge
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Science connects with mathematics, technology, arts and social sciences.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Science is a human activity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It grows through curiosity, creativity, collaboration and learning from mistakes.

examples: Understanding how a mask works needs physics, chemistry, biology and mathematics together.
Tackling climate change or developing medicines requires ideas from several branches of science at once.

importantKeywords.definition: A branch of science such as physics, chemistry or biology.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Biology as the study of living things.

importantKeywords.definition: Combining ideas from more than one field to solve a problem.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Using physics and biology to understand a mask.

importantKeywords.definition: People working and sharing ideas together to build knowledge.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Scientists across countries sharing results.

topics.quickSummary: Science divides into branches for convenience, but the natural world has no such boundaries and real problems like climate change need many disciplines together. Science also connects with mathematics, technology, arts and social sciences. Above all, science is a human activity driven by curiosity, creativity, collaboration and questioning, growing as people test ideas and learn from mistakes. Scientific thinking is valuable for everyone.
revisionNotes: Branches of science are connected, not separate.
Real problems need several disciplines together.
Science links with maths, arts and society.
Science is a creative, collaborative human activity.

chapters.chapter: Cell: The Building Block of Life

topics.topic: The Cell as the Basic Unit of Life
topics.meaning: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms, whether they are single-celled or made of many cells.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: All living organisms are made up of cells, and the cell represents the basic level at which life exists. Some organisms such as bacteria or yeast consist of only one cell and are called unicellular, while others such as plants, fish, birds or humans are made of millions of cells working together and are called multicellular. A group of similar cells performing similar functions forms a tissue, different tissues form an organ, and several organs work together to form an organ system, such as the nasal pores, nasal cavity, trachea and lungs forming the respiratory system. Even when cells are organised into tissues, organs, and organ systems, the cell remains the fundamental unit of structure and function in all living organisms. It is widely accepted that life originated in water, possibly in small water pools such as hot springs, where heat-loving unicellular bacteria called thermophiles still live.
topics.easyExplanation: Every living thing is built from cells, the smallest living building blocks, whether it has just one cell or millions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Cells are the basis of life
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Life exists at the level of the cell, the basic unit of every organism.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Unicellular organisms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Some organisms like bacteria and yeast are made of just one cell.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multicellular organisms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Plants and animals are made of millions of cells working together.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Levels of organisation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Cells form tissues, tissues form organs, and organs form organ systems.

examples: Bacteria and yeast are unicellular, while humans and plants are multicellular.
The nasal pores, nasal cavity, trachea and lungs together form the respiratory system.

importantKeywords.definition: The basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A single bacterium is one cell.

importantKeywords.definition: Made up of only one cell.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Yeast is a unicellular organism.

importantKeywords.definition: Made up of many cells working together.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A human body has millions of cells.

topics.quickSummary: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of life, present in every living organism. Unicellular organisms like bacteria have one cell, while multicellular organisms like humans have millions. Cells form tissues, tissues form organs, and organs form organ systems, yet the cell remains the fundamental unit. Life is thought to have originated in water, where unicellular thermophiles still thrive.
revisionNotes: The cell is the basic unit of life.
Unicellular organisms have one cell; multicellular have many.
Cells form tissues, organs and organ systems.
Life is believed to have originated in water.

topics.meaning: Because cells are smaller than the limit of resolution of the human eye, scientists use light and electron microscopes to study their structure.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The ability of the human eye to see two close objects as separate is limited; at the near point of about 25 cm, two points separated by about 0.1 mm can just be seen as distinct, which is called the limit of resolution of the human eye. A cell is usually too small to be seen by the unaided eye, so cell biologists use microscopes. Robert Hooke first observed cells in 1665 using a self-designed microscope, seeing box-like compartments in cork and naming them cells. Light microscopes use a convex lens or a combination of an objective lens and an eyepiece to magnify objects, and total magnification is the product of the powers of the eyepiece and objective, so two 10X lenses give 100X. Electron microscopes use a beam of electrons instead of light to reveal fine structure at the nanometre scale. Scientists improved microscopes through better resolution, contrast and magnification.
topics.easyExplanation: Cells are too tiny to see with the eye, so we use microscopes that magnify them, including powerful electron microscopes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Limit of the eye
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The human eye cannot separate two points closer than about 0.1 mm.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Hooke's discovery
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Robert Hooke saw box-like cells in cork in 1665 using his own microscope.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Light microscope
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Lenses magnify the object; two 10X lenses give a total magnification of 100X.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Electron microscope
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A beam of electrons reveals fine cell structure at the nanometre scale.

examples: Robert Hooke observed and named cells in 1665 while examining a thin slice of cork.
If both eyepiece and objective lenses have 10X power, the total magnification is 100X.

importantKeywords.definition: The smallest distance at which two points can be seen as separate.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The eye's limit of resolution is about 0.1 mm.

importantKeywords.definition: How much larger an object appears than its real size.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A 100X microscope makes a cell look 100 times bigger.

importantKeywords.definition: An instrument using a beam of electrons to view very fine structures.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Seeing structures at the nanometre scale.

topics.quickSummary: Cells are smaller than the eye's limit of resolution of about 0.1 mm, so microscopes are essential. Robert Hooke first saw and named cells in 1665. Light microscopes use lenses, with total magnification being the product of eyepiece and objective powers, while electron microscopes use electron beams to reveal nanometre-scale detail. Better resolution, contrast and magnification made microscopes powerful tools.
revisionNotes: The eye cannot separate points closer than about 0.1 mm.
Hooke first saw and named cells in 1665.
Light microscope magnification = eyepiece power x objective power.
Electron microscopes show detail at the nanometre scale.

topics.meaning: The cell membrane is a thin, selectively permeable boundary made of lipids and proteins that controls movement of substances, including water by osmosis.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The cell membrane is a thin boundary that surrounds a cell, protects its contents and defines its individuality, and is also called the plasma membrane. It is selectively permeable, allowing some substances to pass while blocking others. Structurally it is about 7 to 10 nanometres thick and made of lipids and proteins, described by the fluid-mosaic model in which a lipid bilayer has proteins embedded in it that act like gatekeepers, with molecules able to move sideways, making the membrane fluid and mosaic-like. Water moves across the membrane by osmosis, the diffusion of water through a selectively permeable membrane from a region of more water and less solute to one of less water and more solute, until concentrations equalise. In a hypotonic solution a cell gains water and swells, in a hypertonic solution it loses water and shrinks, and in an isotonic solution there is no net change.
topics.easyExplanation: The cell membrane is a thin gatekeeping layer that lets some things in and out, and water crosses it by osmosis.

stepByStepExplanation.title: A selectively permeable boundary
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The membrane lets some substances pass while blocking others.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Fluid-mosaic structure
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A lipid bilayer with embedded proteins that can move, making it fluid and mosaic-like.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Osmosis defined
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water moves through the membrane from where there is more water to where there is less.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Effect of solutions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Cells swell in hypotonic, shrink in hypertonic, and stay the same in isotonic solutions.

examples: A potato piece swells in plain water but shrinks in a 20 per cent salt solution because of osmosis.
Water from the soil enters root cells by osmosis across their selectively permeable membranes.

importantKeywords.definition: The thin selectively permeable boundary surrounding a cell.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The layer controlling what enters a cheek cell.

importantKeywords.definition: Diffusion of water through a selectively permeable membrane.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Water entering a root cell from the soil.

importantKeywords.definition: Allowing only certain substances to pass through.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A membrane letting water in but blocking sugar.

topics.quickSummary: The cell membrane, or plasma membrane, is a thin selectively permeable boundary made of a lipid bilayer with embedded proteins, described by the fluid-mosaic model. It controls what enters and leaves the cell. Water crosses it by osmosis, moving from regions of more water to less water through the membrane. Cells swell in hypotonic, shrink in hypertonic, and stay unchanged in isotonic solutions.
revisionNotes: The cell membrane is thin and selectively permeable.
Its structure follows the fluid-mosaic model.
Osmosis is diffusion of water across the membrane.
Cells swell, shrink or stay the same depending on the solution.

topics.meaning: The cell wall is a rigid, permeable outer covering present in plant, fungal and bacterial cells that provides shape, support and protection.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Plant cells, and also fungi and bacteria, have an additional layer outside the cell membrane called the cell wall, which animal cells lack. Because plants usually cannot move from place to place, they need a rigid structure to withstand stresses like wind and rain, and the cell wall helps leaves and flowers stay firm, maintain shape, and keep plants upright. Although rigid, the cell wall is permeable, allowing water and some dissolved minerals to pass through, and together with the selective permeability of the membrane this helps roots absorb water and nutrients. When a plant cell is placed in a concentrated sugar solution it loses water by osmosis, but it does not collapse because the rigid wall maintains its shape; instead the membrane pulls away from the wall as the inner content shrinks. The plant cell wall is made mainly of cellulose, a carbohydrate of many glucose units, which acts as roughage in our diet.

topics.meaning: Cells are classified as prokaryotic, lacking a true nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, or eukaryotic, having both.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Most cells have three basic parts: a selectively permeable plasma membrane, a jelly-like cytoplasm, and a prominent nucleus, with the cytoplasm also containing organelles. A bacterial cell lacks a well-defined nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, and such cells are called prokaryotic, from pro meaning primitive and karyon meaning nucleus, with most cellular activities taking place directly in the cytoplasm. In contrast, plant and animal cells have a well-defined nucleus and several membrane-bound organelles and are called eukaryotic, from eu meaning true. Prokaryotic cells are usually unicellular, typically 1 to 10 micrometres across, and lack membrane-bound organelles and a membrane-bound nucleus, while eukaryotic cells are 10 to 100 micrometres, may be unicellular or multicellular, and possess both. In eukaryotic cells a network of fine fibres called the cytoskeleton provides support and enables movement and internal transport.
topics.easyExplanation: Prokaryotic cells like bacteria have no true nucleus, while eukaryotic cells like plant and animal cells have a real nucleus and organelles.

topics.meaning: The nucleus is a double-membraned organelle that contains chromosomes carrying genetic information and controls the cell's structure and activities.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The nucleus has a double-layered covering called the nuclear membrane, which has pores that allow the transfer of material between the nucleus and the cytoplasm. The nucleolus is a dense round body inside the nucleus where ribosomal subunits are synthesised; these subunits exit to the cytoplasm where a large and a small subunit assemble to form a ribosome. The nucleus contains chromosomes, which become visible as rod-shaped structures only when the cell is about to divide, and chromosomes carry information for the inheritance of characters from parents to the next generation in the form of DNA. A famous experiment with the bacterium Mycoplasma synthesised an exact copy of its DNA in the laboratory and inserted this synthetic DNA into a cell whose own DNA had been removed; the cell then functioned from the new DNA, showing that DNA controls the structure and activities of a cell.
topics.easyExplanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, holding DNA on chromosomes that direct how the cell works and passes on traits.

topics.topic: Endoplasmic Reticulum, Ribosomes and Golgi Apparatus
topics.meaning: Ribosomes make proteins, the endoplasmic reticulum manufactures and transports materials, and the Golgi apparatus packages and dispatches them.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Ribosomes are the sites of protein synthesis and may float freely in the cytoplasm or attach to the endoplasmic reticulum. The Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER) is a large network of membranes that comes in two forms: rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER), which has ribosomes attached to its surface and is involved in protein secretion such as in gland cells like pancreatic cells, and smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER), which lacks surface ribosomes and is involved in the synthesis and storage of fats and hormones. The Golgi apparatus, named after Camillo Golgi, appears as stacks of flattened membranes and is functionally linked to the ER, acting like the cell's packaging and dispatching system, modifying, packaging and sending out materials and forming lysosomes. Together these organelles form a pathway by which proteins and other substances are made, processed and delivered within and outside the cell.
topics.easyExplanation: Ribosomes build proteins, the ER makes and moves materials, and the Golgi packs and ships them like a cell's post office.

topics.meaning: Mitochondria release energy, plastids such as chloroplasts make food in plant cells, and vacuoles store materials and provide support.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Mitochondria are often called the powerhouses of the cell because they release the energy needed for most cellular activities; each mitochondrion is surrounded by two membranes, and inside it glucose and other substances are broken down to release energy. Plastids are found in plant cells, and one type, the chloroplast, contains the green pigment chlorophyll that absorbs sunlight so plants can make food; chloroplasts are double-membrane-bound and contain a semi-fluid stroma. Both mitochondria and plastids have their own DNA and ribosomes and share features with certain bacteria, suggesting a shared evolutionary history. Vacuoles store materials and provide support; a mature plant cell usually has one large central vacuole bounded by a selectively permeable membrane and filled with cell sap that stores water, minerals, sugars and waste, helping keep the cell firm, while animal cells may have small vacuoles for temporary storage.
topics.easyExplanation: Mitochondria make energy, chloroplasts make food using sunlight, and vacuoles store things and keep plant cells firm.

topics.meaning: Cell division is the process by which new cells form, occurring as mitosis for growth and repair and as meiosis for sexual reproduction.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Cells grow and divide continuously, and cell division is the process by which new cells are formed from existing cells. Observing onion root tip cells shows various stages of cell division, with cells in different stages showing different structures. There are two main types of cell division: mitosis and meiosis. Mitosis is the most common type and is important for normal growth, repair, maintenance and asexual reproduction, producing new cells that keep the same number of chromosomes. Meiosis is important for sexual reproduction and for creating genetic diversity. Through mitosis, cells increase in number while maintaining their chromosome number, allowing the body to grow and replace worn-out or damaged cells. The detailed stages of cell division are studied in higher grades, but recognising that cells arise from existing cells through division is a key idea in understanding how living things grow and renew themselves.

topics.topic: Why Plant and Animal Tissues Differ
topics.meaning: Plant and animal tissues differ in structure because plants and animals have different lifestyles, modes of nutrition and ways of growing.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A tissue is a group of similar cells that work together to perform a particular function. Plant and animal tissues differ because plants and animals have very different lifestyles and modes of nutrition. Animals move about and have tissues that help them digest food and move, while plants are usually fixed and have tissues that help them use solar energy to synthesise food. In animals, muscle tissue enables movement and nervous tissue carries messages to different parts of the body, whereas in plants conducting tissues such as xylem transport water and minerals while phloem transports food. Plants also grow only at certain regions through special dividing tissues, while animals generally grow all over. These differences mean the tissues responsible for growth and other functions differ in structure between plants and animals, and the structures of plant and animal tissues are closely related to their specific functions.
topics.easyExplanation: Plant and animal tissues look and work differently because plants stay put and make food, while animals move and eat food.

These structural differences reflect the specific functions each tissue performs.

topics.meaning: Meristematic tissue is made of actively dividing cells, and the apical meristem at root and shoot tips increases the plant's length.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Plant growth requires actively dividing cells that together form a tissue called meristematic tissue. The cells of meristematic tissue are small, have thin cell walls, dense cytoplasm, prominent nuclei and no intercellular spaces, and they lack large vacuoles, which allows them to divide continuously. Plants have meristematic tissue at the tips of their roots and shoots called the apical meristem, which increases the length of the plant. Through continuous cell division, the meristematic tissue adds new cells to the plant body; some newly formed cells remain meristematic while others become specialised to perform specific functions, forming permanent tissues. The apical meristem is responsible for the elongation of roots and shoots, so a plant grows taller and its roots grow deeper through the activity of these dividing cells at the tips. This is why roots stop growing in length when their tips are removed.
topics.easyExplanation: Meristematic tissue is made of dividing cells; the apical meristem at root and shoot tips makes the plant grow longer.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Meristematic tissue is made of cells that divide actively and continuously.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These cells are small, thin-walled, with dense cytoplasm and no vacuoles, so they can keep dividing.

topics.meaning: The lateral meristem increases the girth of stems and roots, while the intercalary meristem at nodes helps growth after branches or leaves form.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Besides growing in length, plants also increase in diameter or girth over time through the activity of meristematic tissues. The lateral meristem is located along the circumference of stems and roots and increases their girth, and its activity produces the annual rings seen on the cut surface of wood, which can be counted to estimate a tree's age. The intercalary meristem is located at the base of leaves or at the nodes of the stem, and it allows growth after branches or leaves have formed, such as the regrowth of grass after it is cut or grazed. Thus plants have three types of meristematic tissues: apical, lateral and intercalary. The apical meristem increases length, the lateral meristem increases girth, and the intercalary meristem helps growth at nodes, and together these meristems account for growth in length, girth and after damage.
topics.easyExplanation: The lateral meristem makes stems thicker, and the intercalary meristem at nodes lets grass and plants regrow after cutting.

topics.meaning: Simple permanent tissues such as parenchyma, collenchyma and sclerenchyma are made of one cell type and provide storage, flexibility and strength.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When meristematic cells stop dividing and become specialised, they form permanent tissues. Simple permanent tissues are made of only one type of cell and include parenchyma, collenchyma and sclerenchyma. Parenchyma consists of living cells with thin walls and intercellular spaces, and it stores food and water and can carry out photosynthesis when it contains chlorophyll, in which case it is called chlorenchyma. Collenchyma consists of living cells with unevenly thickened walls and provides flexibility and mechanical support, allowing parts like young stems and leaf stalks to bend without breaking. Sclerenchyma consists of dead cells with thick, lignified walls and no intercellular spaces, providing strength and rigidity, as in the husk of a coconut. Together these simple permanent tissues give plants storage capacity, flexibility and strength suited to their fixed lifestyle.
topics.easyExplanation: Plants have simple tissues of one cell type: parenchyma stores food, collenchyma gives flexibility, and sclerenchyma gives strength.

topics.meaning: Complex permanent tissues such as xylem and phloem transport substances, while protective tissue covers and protects the plant surface.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Complex permanent tissues are made of more than one type of cell that work together, and the main examples are xylem and phloem, which together form the conducting or vascular tissue of plants. Xylem transports water and dissolved minerals from the roots to the rest of the plant and also provides mechanical support, and much of mature xylem is made of dead cells. Phloem transports food, mainly sugars made in the leaves, to other parts of the plant, and it is made of living cells. Plants also have protective tissue, such as the epidermis that forms a covering on the outer surface of leaves, stems and roots, protecting the plant and reducing water loss. Together, complex permanent tissues handle the long-distance transport of water and food, while protective tissue guards the plant body from the outside environment, both reflecting the fixed lifestyle of plants.
topics.easyExplanation: Xylem carries water up, phloem carries food around, and protective tissue like the epidermis covers and shields the plant.

topics.topic: Epithelial and Connective Tissues in Animals
topics.meaning: Epithelial tissue covers and lines body surfaces, while connective tissue such as blood, bone and cartilage connects, supports and binds body parts.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Animal tissues include epithelial tissue and connective tissue. Epithelial tissue forms a continuous covering or lining over body surfaces, organs and cavities, protecting the underlying parts and controlling the movement of substances, for example lining the skin, mouth and blood vessels. Connective tissue connects, supports and binds different parts of the body and includes blood, bone and cartilage. Blood is a fluid connective tissue that transports substances around the body, bone is a hard connective tissue that forms the framework of the body and supports it, and cartilage is a softer connective tissue found at the ends of bones and in places like the nose and ear, providing smooth surfaces and flexible support. The cells of connective tissues are usually spaced apart and held together by a material between them. Together, epithelial and connective tissues protect, support and hold the animal body together.
topics.easyExplanation: Epithelial tissue covers and lines surfaces, while connective tissues like blood, bone and cartilage support and bind the body.

topics.meaning: Muscular tissue enables body movement through three muscle types, while nervous tissue made of neurons carries messages and controls the body.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Muscular tissue brings about movement in the body, and there are three types of muscles. Skeletal muscles are attached to bones, are under our control (voluntary) and have striations. Smooth muscles are found in internal organs such as the walls of the stomach and blood vessels, are not under our control (involuntary) and lack striations. Cardiac muscles are found only in the heart, are involuntary, have a single nucleus and show faint striations. Muscles, whether voluntary or involuntary, cannot act on their own; they are controlled by nervous tissue, which forms the body's control and communication system. The cells of nervous tissue are called neurons, and each neuron has three main parts: the cell body, dendrites that receive signals from other neurons, and a long fibre called the axon that carries messages. For example, during exercise the brain coordinates the muscles through the nervous tissue.
topics.easyExplanation: Muscle tissue moves the body using three muscle types, and nervous tissue made of neurons carries signals and controls movement.

topics.meaning: The musculoskeletal system of bones, muscles, joints, cartilage, tendons and ligaments lets us stand, move, keep posture and protect organs.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The musculoskeletal system is made up of bones, muscles, joints, cartilage, tendons and ligaments, and it helps us stand upright, move, maintain posture and protect delicate organs. This system functions under the control of the nervous system. Muscles pull on bones to produce movement and are attached to bones by strong, flexible bands called tendons; when a muscle contracts, the tendon transmits this force to the bone, resulting in movement at a joint. Ligaments are tough bands that connect bone to bone and hold joints together. On average, bones make up a notable share of body weight, with adults having roughly 12 to 15 per cent bone mass, while muscle mass differs between individuals and between males and females. The musculoskeletal system thus combines the framework of bones with the pulling action of muscles and the connections of tendons and ligaments to allow controlled movement.

topics.meaning: A joint is a junction between bones, and different joint types such as ball-and-socket, hinge, pivot and fixed joints allow different kinds of movement.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A joint is a junction between two or more bones, and joints allow movement, although they cannot move the bones on their own; muscles cause the bones to move at joints. The type of movement possible depends on the type of joint. A ball-and-socket joint, such as the shoulder joint where the rounded head of the arm bone fits into a shallow hollow of the shoulder bone, allows forward, backward, sideways and circular movement. A hinge joint, such as the elbow or knee, allows movement mainly in one direction, like the opening and closing of a door. A pivot joint allows rotation, such as the joint that lets the head turn from side to side. A fixed joint allows no movement, such as the joints between the bones of the skull. Thus the variety of joints in the body gives different parts their characteristic ranges of movement.
topics.easyExplanation: Joints are where bones meet; ball-and-socket joints move all ways, hinge joints one way, pivot joints rotate, and fixed joints do not move.

topics.meaning: The skeletal system is the framework of bones that supports the body, gives it shape, protects organs and works with muscles to allow movement.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The skeletal system is the framework of bones in the body, and it serves several important functions: it supports the body and gives it shape, protects delicate internal organs, and works together with muscles to bring about movement. Bones are a hard connective tissue that forms this framework. The skull protects the brain, the ribs protect the heart and lungs, and the backbone supports the body and protects the spinal cord. Bones are connected at joints, and muscles attached to bones by tendons pull on them to produce movement. Cartilage is present at the ends of bones and in places such as the nose and ear, providing smooth surfaces and flexible support that reduce friction and cushion the joints. Together with muscles, joints, tendons, ligaments and cartilage, the skeletal system forms the larger musculoskeletal system that allows the body to stand, move and stay protected.

topics.meaning: The position of an object is described relative to a chosen fixed reference point, and motion means a change of position over time.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To describe where an object is, we first need to specify a fixed point called the reference point or origin. The position of an object is its location described with respect to this reference point, and we measure how far the object is from the reference point along a chosen direction. An object is said to be in motion when its position changes with time relative to the reference point, and at rest when its position does not change. Because position depends on the chosen reference point, the same object may appear to be moving relative to one reference point and at rest relative to another. For example, a passenger sitting in a moving train is at rest relative to the train but in motion relative to the platform. Choosing a clear reference point is therefore the starting step in describing the motion of any object in a straight line.
topics.easyExplanation: We describe where something is by measuring from a fixed point, and motion means that distance changes over time.

topics.meaning: Distance is the total path length travelled, while displacement is the net change in position with both magnitude and direction.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When an object moves, the total distance travelled is the entire length of the path it covers, regardless of direction. Displacement, in contrast, is the net change in the position of an object between its starting and final positions. The magnitude of displacement is the straight-line distance between the object's positions at the two instants, and its direction is specified from the starting position towards the final position. Distance is a quantity that has only magnitude, while displacement requires specifying both magnitude and direction. For an athlete who runs 100 m forward and then 60 m back, the total distance travelled is 160 m, but the displacement is only 40 m in the forward direction. Thus distance and the magnitude of displacement are not always equal; they are equal only when the object moves in a straight line in one direction without reversing.
topics.easyExplanation: Distance is how much ground you cover, while displacement is how far you end up from the start, with a direction.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Distance and the magnitude of displacement are equal only for straight one-way motion.

topics.meaning: Scalar quantities have only magnitude, while vector quantities require both a magnitude and a direction.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Physical quantities can be grouped according to whether direction matters in describing them. Quantities that are described completely by their numerical value, or magnitude, alone are called scalars; examples include distance, speed, mass and time. Quantities that require specifying both a magnitude and a direction are called vectors; examples include displacement, velocity and acceleration. For instance, saying an object covered 40 m describes a distance, which is a scalar, but saying it was displaced 40 m towards the east describes a vector, because direction is included. Recognising whether a quantity is a scalar or a vector is important because the two kinds of quantities behave differently when combined. This distinction helps us describe motion precisely, since some descriptions need only a number while others need both a number and a direction to be complete.
topics.easyExplanation: A scalar is just a number with a unit, like speed, while a vector also needs a direction, like velocity.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Scalars are fully described by a number and unit.

topics.meaning: Speed is the distance travelled per unit time, while velocity is the displacement per unit time and includes direction.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Speed tells us how fast an object moves and is defined as the distance travelled per unit time, making it a scalar quantity with the SI unit metre per second. Average speed is the total distance travelled divided by the total time taken. Velocity is the rate of change of displacement, or displacement per unit time, and because displacement has direction, velocity is a vector quantity that includes direction. Average velocity is the total displacement divided by the total time, and for motion in a straight line in one direction it can also be taken as the average of the initial and final velocities. While speed depends on the total path length, velocity depends on the net change in position, so an object that returns to its starting point has zero average velocity even though its average speed is not zero. Both speed and velocity describe how quickly motion happens.
topics.easyExplanation: Speed is how fast you go; velocity is how fast you go in a particular direction.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Speed is the distance travelled per unit time, a scalar.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Velocity is displacement per unit time, a vector with direction.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Average velocity is total displacement divided by total time.

topics.meaning: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time, and it is a vector quantity.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When the velocity of an object changes, the object is said to be accelerating. Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity with time, that is, the change in velocity divided by the time taken for that change. Because velocity is a vector, acceleration is also a vector quantity, and its SI unit is metre per second squared. Average acceleration is found by subtracting the initial velocity from the final velocity and dividing by the time interval. If the velocity increases in the direction of motion, the acceleration is positive, and if the velocity decreases, the acceleration is negative, which is sometimes called retardation or deceleration. Uniform acceleration occurs when the velocity changes by equal amounts in equal intervals of time. A common example is free fall, where an object falling near the Earth accelerates due to gravity at about 9.8 m s-2 in the downward direction.
topics.easyExplanation: Acceleration tells how quickly velocity changes; speeding up gives positive and slowing down gives negative acceleration.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Acceleration is the change in velocity divided by the time taken.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Speeding up gives positive acceleration; slowing down gives negative acceleration.

topics.meaning: A position-time graph shows how an object's position changes with time, and its slope gives the object's velocity.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Motion can be represented visually using graphs, which make it easier to understand how quantities change. A position-time graph plots the position of an object on the vertical axis against time on the horizontal axis. The slope of this graph gives the velocity of the object, because slope is the change in position divided by the change in time. For an object at rest, the position-time graph is a horizontal straight line, since position does not change with time. For an object moving with uniform velocity, the graph is a straight line sloping upward, and a steeper slope means a greater velocity. For non-uniform motion, where velocity changes, the graph is a curve. Reading position-time graphs lets us find an object's position at any time and determine its velocity from the slope, making graphs a powerful tool for analysing motion.
topics.easyExplanation: A position-time graph plots where an object is over time, and how steep the line is tells you how fast it moves.

topics.meaning: A velocity-time graph shows how velocity changes with time, with its slope giving acceleration and the area under it giving displacement.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A velocity-time graph plots the velocity of an object on the vertical axis against time on the horizontal axis. The slope of a velocity-time graph gives the acceleration of the object, since slope is the change in velocity divided by the change in time. For example, the average acceleration over an interval is found by taking the change in velocity between two points on the line and dividing by the time interval. For an object moving with uniform velocity, the graph is a horizontal straight line, while for uniformly accelerated motion the graph is a straight line sloping upward. In addition, the area enclosed under a velocity-time graph between two instants gives the displacement of the object during that time interval. Velocity-time graphs are therefore very useful because they reveal both the acceleration, from the slope, and the displacement, from the area, in a single picture.
topics.easyExplanation: A velocity-time graph shows how speed changes over time; its slope is acceleration and the area under it is displacement.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Velocity is plotted on the vertical axis against time on the horizontal axis.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The slope equals change in velocity over change in time, which is acceleration.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A horizontal line means constant velocity and zero acceleration.

topics.meaning: The three kinematic equations relate initial velocity, final velocity, acceleration, time and displacement for uniformly accelerated straight-line motion.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: For an object moving in a straight line with uniform acceleration, three equations of motion relate its initial velocity, final velocity, acceleration, time and displacement, allowing us to solve for an unknown quantity when the others are known. The first equation relates final velocity to initial velocity, acceleration and time. The second equation relates displacement to initial velocity, time and acceleration. The third equation relates the square of the final velocity to the square of the initial velocity, acceleration and displacement, and does not involve time. These kinematic equations can be derived from the definitions of velocity and acceleration and from velocity-time graphs. They are widely used to describe situations such as a freely falling body, a braking vehicle, or an accelerating train, as long as the acceleration is uniform. Knowing any three of the quantities allows the others to be calculated using the appropriate equation.

topics.meaning: Uniform circular motion is motion along a circular path at constant speed, in which the direction and hence the velocity continually change.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Motion is not always in a straight line; objects can also move in a plane, such as along a circular path. Uniform circular motion is the motion of an object moving along a circular path at a constant speed. Although the speed remains constant, the direction of motion changes continuously because the object keeps moving along the curve. Since velocity is a vector that depends on direction, the velocity of the object changes continuously even though its speed is constant, which means the object is accelerating. At every point on the circle, the direction of motion is along the tangent to the circle at that point. Everyday examples of uniform circular motion include the tip of a clock's hand, a stone whirled at the end of a string, and a satellite moving around the Earth. Uniform circular motion shows that a change in direction alone, without a change in speed, still counts as a change in velocity.

topics.meaning: A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout, while a heterogeneous mixture has visibly different parts.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Mixtures can be classified by how uniformly their components are mixed. A mixture in which the components are uniformly mixed so that no boundaries are visible and the composition is the same throughout is called a homogeneous mixture, also known as a solution; examples include sugar dissolved in water or salt dissolved in water. In contrast, a mixture in which the components remain visibly separate and the composition is not the same throughout is called a heterogeneous mixture, such as a stirred mixture of sand and water or a mixture of oil and water. In a homogeneous mixture the particles of the components are evenly distributed and cannot be distinguished, while in a heterogeneous mixture different parts can be told apart. Classifying a mixture as homogeneous or heterogeneous helps decide which method should be used to separate its components.

topics.meaning: A solution is a homogeneous mixture in which a solute dissolves in a solvent.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Solutions are homogeneous mixtures formed when a solute, the substance that gets dissolved, is mixed with a solvent, the substance that dissolves the solute. In a sugar solution, sugar is the solute and water is the solvent, while in a salt solution, salt is the solute and water the solvent. The solvent is usually the component present in the larger amount. In a solution the solute particles are so small that they are evenly spread among the solvent particles and cannot be seen or filtered out, and the solution remains clear and stable. Solutions are not limited to solids dissolved in liquids; air, for example, is a solution of gases. The components of a solution stay uniformly mixed and do not settle on standing. Understanding the roles of solute and solvent is the basis for describing the concentration and properties of solutions.

topics.meaning: Concentration is the amount of solute present in a given amount of solvent or solution.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The concentration of a solution is the amount of solute dissolved in a given amount of solvent or solution, telling us how much solute is present. One common way to express concentration is as the mass of solute per total mass of solution, expressed as a percentage, called mass percentage; for example, dissolving 10 g of salt in 90 g of water gives a 100 g solution that is 10 per cent salt by mass. Another way is mass of solute per volume of solution, where a certain mass of solute is present in 100 millilitres of solution. Concentration tells us whether a solution is dilute, with little solute, or concentrated, with much solute. Expressing concentration precisely is important in everyday life and medicine, such as a 0.9 per cent saline solution, and in agriculture, such as preparing a pesticide solution of a known strength. Knowing concentration lets us prepare and compare solutions accurately.

topics.meaning: Solubility is the maximum amount of a solute that dissolves in a fixed quantity of solvent at a given temperature, beyond which the solution is saturated.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Solubility is the maximum amount of a solute that can be dissolved in a fixed quantity of solvent, usually 100 mL or 100 g, at a given temperature. A solution that contains the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve at a particular temperature is called a saturated solution, and no more solute will dissolve in it at that temperature. Temperature is important because the solubility of a solid solute in a liquid usually increases as temperature rises, while the solubility of gases in liquids generally decreases as temperature rises. A graph of solubility versus temperature is called a solubility curve, and different substances have different curves, showing that each substance dissolves to a different extent at each temperature. Solubility is a useful property that can be used to separate and purify substances, since cooling a hot saturated solution causes excess solute to come out.

topics.meaning: Crystallization is a method of obtaining pure crystals of a solid from its saturated solution, based on differences in solubility at different temperatures.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Crystallization is the process of forming crystals from a saturated solution, and it is used to separate and purify solids. The principle of purification by crystallization is based on the difference in the solubility of a substance at different temperatures. To crystallize a substance such as copper sulfate, a saturated solution is prepared in hot water, filtered while hot to remove insoluble impurities, and then allowed to cool slowly. As the solution cools, its ability to hold dissolved solute decreases, so the excess solute separates out as pure crystals, while soluble impurities remain dissolved in the solution. Crystallization gives purer crystals than simple evaporation, because evaporation may leave impurities mixed with the solid, whereas in crystallization impurities stay behind in the liquid. This method is widely used to purify substances and to obtain well-formed crystals from a solution.
topics.easyExplanation: Crystallization makes pure solid crystals by cooling a hot saturated solution so the extra solute separates out cleanly.

topics.meaning: Two immiscible liquids that do not mix can be separated using a separating funnel, which exploits their difference in density.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Some liquids do not mix with each other and form separate layers; such liquids are called immiscible liquids, for example oil and water. A mixture of two immiscible liquids can be separated using a separating funnel, which works on the basis of the difference in their densities. The mixture is poured into the separating funnel and allowed to stand, so that the denser liquid settles at the bottom while the lighter liquid floats on top, forming two clear layers. The stopcock at the bottom of the funnel is then opened to let the lower, denser liquid run out into a container, and it is closed just as the upper liquid reaches the stopcock, so the two liquids are collected separately. This method is used wherever two liquids form distinct layers and do not dissolve in each other, separating them cleanly by allowing the denser one to be drained off first.

topics.meaning: Sublimation is the process of separating a solid that changes directly into vapour on heating, without becoming a liquid, from a mixture.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Some solids change directly from the solid state into the vapour state on heating, without passing through the liquid state, and this process is called sublimation. This property can be used to separate such a substance from a mixture with another solid that does not sublime. When the mixture is heated, the subliming solid turns into vapour and rises, while the non-subliming solid remains behind. The vapour is then cooled on a cold surface, where it changes back into the solid state and can be collected separately, leaving the other component in the original container. Substances such as ammonium chloride, camphor and naphthalene undergo sublimation. Sublimation is therefore useful for separating a mixture of two solids when only one of them sublimes, since the subliming component can be removed as vapour and recovered, while the other stays behind as a residue.

topics.meaning: A suspension has large visible particles that settle on standing, while a colloid has tiny particles that stay dispersed and do not settle.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Heterogeneous mixtures of small particles in a medium can be classified as suspensions or colloids. A suspension is a heterogeneous mixture in which the solute particles are large enough to be seen and do not dissolve, so they remain suspended and settle down when the mixture is left undisturbed; an example is chalk powder or sand stirred in water. A colloid is a mixture in which the particles are much smaller than in a suspension but larger than in a true solution, so they remain evenly spread throughout the medium and do not settle on standing; the particles are too small to be seen individually, yet the mixture appears uniform. Colloids look homogeneous to the naked eye but are actually heterogeneous at the microscopic level. Examples of colloids include milk and fog. The size of the particles thus distinguishes true solutions, colloids and suspensions, and affects how stable each mixture is.

topics.meaning: The Tyndall effect is the scattering of a beam of light by the particles of a colloid, which makes the path of the light visible.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Tyndall effect is the scattering of light by the particles present in a colloid. When a beam of light passes through a colloid, the colloidal particles are large enough to scatter the light, making the path of the beam visible, whereas in a true solution the particles are too small to scatter light and the beam's path cannot be seen. This effect can be observed when a beam of sunlight enters a dark room through a small hole and the path of the light becomes visible due to scattering by dust particles in the air, or when light passes through fog or mist. The Tyndall effect is therefore a useful way to tell a colloid apart from a true solution, since a colloid shows the effect while a true solution does not. The visibility of the light path indicates the presence of suspended colloidal particles in the medium.

topics.meaning: A force is a push or pull that can change the state of motion, speed, direction or shape of an object.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A force is a push or a pull exerted on an object, and it can produce several effects. A force can set a stationary object into motion, stop a moving object, change the speed of a moving object, change its direction of motion, or change the shape and size of an object. Force is a vector quantity, meaning it has both magnitude and direction, and its SI unit is the newton. To describe a force fully, we need to state how strong it is and the direction in which it acts. Forces act everywhere in daily life, such as kicking a ball, pushing a door, or stretching a rubber band. The magnitude of a force can be measured using instruments based on the stretching of a spring. Understanding force is fundamental, because force is what causes changes in the motion or shape of objects around us.

topics.meaning: Balanced forces cancel out and produce no change in motion, while unbalanced forces produce a net force that changes an object's motion.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When several forces act on an object at the same time, their combined effect is called the net force. Forces are said to be balanced when they cancel each other out so that the net force is zero, and in this case the state of motion of the object does not change; a stationary object stays at rest and a moving object continues with the same velocity. Forces are said to be unbalanced when they do not cancel out, leaving a non-zero net force, and this net force changes the object's state of motion by starting it, stopping it, speeding it up, slowing it down or changing its direction. For example, in a tug of war, when both teams pull equally the rope does not move because the forces are balanced, but when one team pulls harder, the unbalanced force moves the rope towards them. A change in motion always requires an unbalanced force.
topics.easyExplanation: Balanced forces cancel and nothing changes, but unbalanced forces leave a net push that changes how an object moves.
