topics.meaning: Friction is a force that opposes the relative motion between two surfaces in contact.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Friction is a force that opposes the relative motion between two surfaces that are in contact, and it always acts in the direction opposite to the motion or attempted motion. Friction is the reason a moving object slows down and eventually stops when no other force acts on it, because friction continually opposes its motion. Friction arises from the irregularities of the surfaces in contact, which interlock and resist sliding. Although friction opposes motion and can waste energy, it is also useful and often necessary; without friction we could not walk, vehicles could not grip the road, and objects could not be held. The amount of friction depends on the nature of the surfaces in contact and on how hard the surfaces are pressed together. Recognising friction as an opposing force is important for understanding why objects do not keep moving forever and why a force is needed to keep them moving.

topics.meaning: Newton's first law states that an object stays at rest or in uniform motion in a straight line unless acted on by an unbalanced force.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Newton's first law of motion states that an object continues in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line unless an unbalanced external force acts on it. This means that an object at rest will remain at rest, and an object moving with a constant velocity will continue to move with the same speed and direction, until an unbalanced force changes its state. The natural tendency of objects to resist a change in their state of motion is called inertia, and the mass of an object is a measure of its inertia, so heavier objects have greater inertia and are harder to start or stop. This law explains the jerk felt when a bus suddenly starts or stops, because our body tends to keep its earlier state of motion. Because of this property, Newton's first law is also known as the law of inertia.
topics.easyExplanation: An object keeps doing what it is doing, staying still or moving steadily, until an unbalanced force changes it.

topics.meaning: Newton's second law states that the net force on an object equals its mass times its acceleration.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Newton's second law of motion gives a relationship between force, mass and acceleration, stating that the acceleration produced in an object is directly proportional to the net force applied and inversely proportional to its mass. This is expressed as net force equals mass times acceleration, so a larger force produces a larger acceleration, and for the same force a heavier object accelerates less. This law explains why pushing a lighter object makes it speed up more easily than pushing a heavier one with the same force, and it allows us to calculate the force needed to produce a given change in motion. The SI unit of force, the newton, is defined through this law as the force that gives a mass of one kilogram an acceleration of one metre per second squared. Newton's second law gives a precise, measurable way to connect force with the change of motion it produces.

topics.meaning: Newton's third law states that to every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Newton's third law of motion states that to every action there is an equal and opposite reaction, meaning that when one object exerts a force on a second object, the second object exerts an equal force in the opposite direction on the first. These two forces are always equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, and they act on two different objects, not on the same object, which is why they do not cancel each other. For example, when we walk, we push the ground backward with our feet and the ground pushes us forward with an equal force, allowing us to move ahead. Similarly, when a swimmer pushes the water backward, the water pushes the swimmer forward, and a gun recoils backward when it pushes a bullet forward. Recognising that action and reaction forces act on different bodies is essential for correctly applying this law to real situations.

topics.topic: Forces on a System of Objects
topics.meaning: When objects are connected or pushed together, they can be treated as a single system, and the net external force determines their common acceleration.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When two or more objects move together, such as connected blocks or objects pushed in contact, they can be treated as a single system. The net external force acting on the whole system determines the common acceleration of all objects in it, found by dividing the net external force by the total mass of the system. The internal forces between the objects, which are action-reaction pairs, do not affect the motion of the system as a whole because they cancel within the system. Once the common acceleration is known, the force on any individual object within the system can be found by considering that object alone and applying Newton's second law to it. Treating connected objects as a system simplifies problems, such as finding the acceleration of two coupled railway carriages pulled by an engine or the force between two boxes pushed together along the ground.

topics.topic: Measuring the Magnitude of a Force
topics.meaning: The magnitude of a force can be measured using a spring balance, which relies on the stretching of a spring being proportional to the applied force.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Since force is a vector with magnitude and direction, measuring its magnitude is important. The magnitude of a force can be measured using a spring balance, which works on the principle that the amount a spring stretches is proportional to the force applied to it within certain limits. When a force pulls on the spring, the spring extends, and the greater the force, the greater the extension, so a scale attached to the spring can be marked to read the force directly in newtons. To measure a force, the object or pull is attached to the hook of the spring balance, and the reading on the scale, taken when the pointer is steady, gives the magnitude of the force. This method lets us compare forces and assign each a numerical value in standard units. Being able to measure the magnitude of a force allows forces to be used quantitatively in the laws of motion.

topics.meaning: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist a change in its state of motion, and mass is the measure of an object's inertia.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Inertia is the natural tendency of an object to resist any change in its state of rest or of uniform motion, and it is the property highlighted by Newton's first law. Because of inertia, an object at rest tends to stay at rest and an object in motion tends to keep moving with the same velocity. The mass of an object is a measure of its inertia, so an object with more mass has more inertia and is harder to start moving, stop or change direction. This is why a loaded truck is harder to push or stop than an empty cart. Inertia explains many everyday observations, such as the forward jerk when a bus stops suddenly, dust flying off when a carpet is beaten, and a coin dropping into a glass when the card beneath it is flicked away. Mass is a scalar quantity measured in kilograms, and the greater the mass, the greater the inertia.

topics.topic: Work Done by a Constant Force
topics.meaning: Work is done when a force acting on an object produces a displacement, and it equals the force times the displacement in the direction of the force.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In science, work has a precise meaning: work is said to be done when a force acts on an object and the object moves in the direction of the force. The work done by a constant force is equal to the product of the magnitude of the force and the displacement of the object in the direction of the force. Work is a scalar quantity, and its SI unit is the joule, where one joule is the work done when a force of one newton moves an object through one metre in the direction of the force. For work to be done, both a force and a displacement are needed, and the displacement must have a component along the direction of the force. This scientific meaning differs from everyday usage, where simply holding a heavy bag is called work even though no displacement occurs. Calculating work lets us measure how much a force achieves in moving an object.

topics.meaning: Work can be positive, negative or zero depending on the direction of the force relative to the displacement of the object.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The work done by a force can be positive, negative or zero, depending on the direction of the displacement relative to the force. Work is positive when the force and the displacement are in the same direction, such as when a force pushes a wheelchair forward and it moves forward. Work is negative when the force acts opposite to the displacement, such as the force of friction acting opposite to a moving object's motion. Work done is zero when there is no displacement, even though a force is applied, or when the displacement is perpendicular to the force, so the force has no component along the displacement. For example, holding a heavy object without moving it does zero work, and carrying a load horizontally does zero work against gravity since gravity acts downward while motion is horizontal. Recognising the sign of work helps us understand whether energy is being given to or taken from an object.

topics.meaning: The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The work-energy theorem connects the work done on an object with the change in its energy of motion. It states that the net work done by all the forces acting on an object is equal to the change in its kinetic energy. If positive net work is done on an object, its kinetic energy increases and it speeds up, while if negative net work is done, its kinetic energy decreases and it slows down. For example, when a force does work on a moving ball, the ball's kinetic energy changes by an amount equal to that work. This theorem provides a powerful way to relate the forces and displacements acting on an object to changes in its speed without needing to know all the details of the motion. It shows that work is a means of transferring energy to or from an object, linking the ideas of work and energy directly.

topics.meaning: Energy is the capacity to do work and exists in many forms, such as mechanical, heat, light, sound, chemical and electrical energy, which can be converted into one another.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Energy is the capacity to do work, and an object that can do work is said to possess energy. Energy exists in many different forms, including mechanical energy, heat energy, light energy, sound energy, chemical energy and electrical energy. One of the most important ideas about energy is that it can be transformed from one form into another. For example, in a torch, chemical energy in the cells is converted into electrical energy and then into light energy, while in our bodies the chemical energy in food is converted into the energy of movement and heat. Like work, energy is a scalar quantity measured in joules. Energy from the Sun, in the form of light and heat, is the source of most of the energy used on Earth. Recognising the different forms of energy and how they change into one another is central to understanding the natural world and the technology we use.

topics.meaning: Kinetic energy is the energy an object possesses due to its motion, and it increases with the object's mass and the square of its speed.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Kinetic energy is the energy that an object possesses because of its motion, and any moving object can do work by virtue of its motion. The kinetic energy of an object depends on its mass and its speed: it is directly proportional to the mass and to the square of the speed, so doubling the speed makes the kinetic energy four times as large, while doubling the mass only doubles it. This is why a fast-moving object, such as a speeding cricket ball or a moving vehicle, carries a large amount of energy and can do significant work or damage on impact. Kinetic energy is a scalar quantity measured in joules. According to the work-energy theorem, the kinetic energy gained by an object equals the work done on it. Understanding kinetic energy helps explain why speed matters so much for the energy and impact of moving objects.

topics.meaning: Potential energy is the energy possessed by an object due to its position or configuration, such as height above the ground or a stretched state.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Potential energy is the energy that an object possesses because of its position or its configuration. The most familiar form is gravitational potential energy, which an object has because of its height above the ground; the higher an object is raised, the more potential energy it gains, and this energy is equal to the work done in raising the object against gravity. The gravitational potential energy of an object depends on its mass, the height to which it is raised, and the acceleration due to gravity. Energy is also stored when an object's shape is changed, such as in a stretched or compressed spring or a drawn bow, which is a form of elastic potential energy. A raised hammer, water stored behind a dam, and a stretched rubber band all store potential energy that can later do work. Potential energy is a scalar quantity measured in joules and can be converted into kinetic energy.

topics.meaning: The total mechanical energy, the sum of kinetic and potential energy, stays constant for a freely falling object when only gravity acts.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Mechanical energy is the sum of the kinetic energy and the potential energy of an object. The principle of conservation of mechanical energy states that when only the force of gravity acts on an object, the total mechanical energy remains constant, although it may change from one form to the other. For a freely falling object, as it falls its height decreases so its potential energy decreases, while its speed increases so its kinetic energy increases by the same amount, keeping the sum constant. At the top of the fall the energy is mostly potential, and just before hitting the ground it is mostly kinetic, but the total stays the same throughout. This is a special case of the broader law of conservation of energy, which states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed but only transformed from one form to another. Conservation of mechanical energy lets us analyse motion without tracking every instant.

topics.meaning: Power is the rate of doing work or the rate of transferring energy, and its SI unit is the watt.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Power is the rate at which work is done or the rate at which energy is transferred or transformed. Two people may do the same amount of work, but the one who does it in a shorter time has greater power. Power is calculated by dividing the work done, or the energy transferred, by the time taken to do it. The SI unit of power is the watt, where one watt is equal to one joule of work done in one second; a larger unit, the kilowatt, equals one thousand watts. Power describes not just how much work is done but how quickly it is done, which is important for machines and appliances. For example, a powerful engine can do a given amount of work faster than a weaker one. Understanding power lets us compare how rapidly different devices and people can do work or use energy.

topics.meaning: A simple machine makes work easier by changing the size or direction of a force, and its mechanical advantage is the ratio of load to effort.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Simple machines are devices that help us do work more easily by changing the magnitude or direction of an applied force. The force we apply to a machine is called the effort, and the force that needs to be overcome is called the load. To describe how a machine helps, we define its mechanical advantage as the ratio of the load to the effort, so a machine with a mechanical advantage greater than one lets us overcome a large load with a smaller effort. Three common simple machines are the pulley, the inclined plane and the lever. A simple machine does not reduce the total work needed; instead, it makes a task feel easier by allowing a smaller force to be applied over a greater distance, or by changing the direction in which the force is applied. Understanding mechanical advantage lets us see how everyday tools make difficult tasks more manageable.

topics.meaning: The pulley changes the direction of a force, the inclined plane reduces the effort to raise a load, and the lever turns about a fulcrum to ease work.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Three common simple machines work in different ways. A pulley is a wheel with a groove that guides a rope; a fixed pulley does not reduce the magnitude of the force but changes its direction, so a load can be raised by pulling downward, which is more convenient. An inclined plane is a sloping surface used to raise a load to a height with less effort than lifting it straight up, although the load must be moved over a greater distance along the slope, which is why escape ramps use inclined planes to slow runaway trucks. A lever is a rigid bar that turns about a fixed point called the fulcrum; by applying effort at one point, a load at another point can be moved, and the positions of the effort, load and fulcrum determine the mechanical advantage. Together, these machines show different ways of making work easier by changing the direction or reducing the effort needed.

topics.meaning: Dalton's atomic theory was the first scientific theory to propose that all matter is made of tiny indivisible particles called atoms.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In 1808 John Dalton proposed his atomic theory, which was the first scientific theory about the nature of matter. According to Dalton, all matter is made up of very small particles called atoms, which cannot be broken down into smaller parts. The theory proposed that atoms of a given element are identical in their properties, while atoms of different elements differ from one another, and that atoms combine in simple whole-number ratios to form compounds. Dalton's atomic theory successfully explained the laws of chemical combination, such as the law of conservation of mass and the law of constant proportions, which made it a powerful foundation for chemistry. Although later discoveries showed that atoms are not truly indivisible and can be broken into smaller particles, Dalton's theory was a crucial first step, and scientists built on it to develop more detailed models of the atom.

topics.topic: Discovery of the Electron and Thomson's Model
topics.meaning: J. J. Thomson discovered the negatively charged electron and proposed a model of the atom as a sphere of positive charge with electrons embedded in it.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In 1897 J. J. Thomson studied the conduction of electric current through gases in a cathode ray tube and discovered tiny negatively charged particles, later called electrons, which have a much smaller mass than atoms. He found that these particles were the same regardless of the material of the cathode or the gas used, showing that electrons are present in all atoms. The charge of an electron is taken as minus one as a relative value. Faced with the puzzle that atoms are electrically neutral yet contain negative electrons, Thomson proposed that the atom is a sphere of positive charge with electrons distributed throughout it, a model often compared to plums embedded in a pudding. This was the first genuine attempt to describe the internal structure of the atom, though it was later replaced when it failed to explain the results of the gold foil experiment.

topics.topic: The Gold Foil Experiment and Rutherford's Model
topics.meaning: The gold foil experiment showed that the atom has a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus at its centre, leading to Rutherford's nuclear model.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In 1911 Geiger and Marsden, working under Ernest Rutherford, tested Thomson's model with the gold foil experiment, aiming a narrow beam of positively charged alpha particles at an extremely thin sheet of gold foil. According to Thomson's model, the positive charge was spread throughout the atom, so the alpha particles were expected to pass through or be deflected only slightly. To their surprise, while most alpha particles passed straight through, some were deflected by large angles and a very few bounced straight back, a change in path called scattering. This showed that most of the atom is empty space, but that there is a tiny, dense, positively charged region at the centre, which Rutherford called the nucleus, where almost all the mass and the positive charge are concentrated. Rutherford proposed that electrons revolve around this nucleus, giving the nuclear model of the atom.

topics.meaning: The nucleus of an atom contains positively charged protons and uncharged neutrons, which together account for almost all the atom's mass.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: After the discovery of the nucleus, scientists identified the particles that make it up. The proton is a positively charged particle found in the nucleus, and its charge is equal in size but opposite to that of the electron, while its mass is much greater than that of an electron. Because atoms are electrically neutral, the number of protons in an atom equals the number of electrons, so their charges balance. Later, the neutron was discovered as a particle in the nucleus that carries no charge and has a mass nearly equal to that of a proton. Since neutrons are uncharged, they can pass easily into the nucleus. The protons and neutrons together make up almost all of the mass of an atom, because electrons are very light. These three particles, protons, neutrons and electrons, are the fundamental subatomic particles that make up atoms.

topics.meaning: Each element is represented by a symbol, usually one or two letters derived from its name, used as a shorthand in chemistry worldwide.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Each element is represented by a short symbol, which acts as an internationally agreed shorthand for that element. A symbol is usually the first letter of the element's name written as a capital, such as H for hydrogen and O for oxygen, or the first two letters with the first capital and the second small, such as Ca for calcium and Cl for chlorine. Some elements have symbols derived from their Latin or older names rather than their English names, such as Na for sodium from natrium and Fe for iron from ferrum. These symbols are used by scientists across the world so that elements can be written and recognised in the same way everywhere, regardless of language. Symbols form the basis for writing chemical formulae and equations in a compact and universal manner. Knowing the symbols of common elements is essential for understanding and communicating chemistry.

topics.meaning: The atomic number is the number of protons in an atom, and the mass number is the total number of protons and neutrons in its nucleus.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Atoms of different elements are distinguished by the number of protons they contain. The atomic number of an element is the number of protons present in the nucleus of its atom, and since atoms are neutral, it also equals the number of electrons. The atomic number is what defines an element, so all atoms of a given element have the same atomic number, and it is usually denoted by the letter Z. The mass number is the total number of protons and neutrons present in the nucleus of an atom, usually denoted by the letter A, because protons and neutrons together account for almost all of the atom's mass. The number of neutrons in an atom can be found by subtracting the atomic number from the mass number. Together, the atomic number and mass number describe the composition of an atom's nucleus and are central to identifying elements and their atoms.

topics.meaning: Electrons are arranged around the nucleus in energy shells filled according to definite rules, with the outermost shell determining chemical behaviour.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Electrons in an atom are not scattered randomly but are arranged around the nucleus in distinct energy levels or shells, which are filled following definite rules. The shells are numbered outward from the nucleus, and each shell can hold only a limited maximum number of electrons. Electrons are filled into the shells starting from the one nearest the nucleus and moving outward, so the inner shells are filled before the outer ones. The arrangement of electrons in these shells is called the electronic configuration of the atom. The outermost shell of an atom is especially important because the electrons in it, called valence electrons, determine how the atom combines with other atoms. Building up atoms by adding electrons shell by shell helps explain the structure of different elements and predicts their chemical behaviour, since the way the outer shell is filled controls how reactive an atom is.

topics.meaning: Valency is the combining capacity of an atom, determined by the number of electrons in its outermost shell.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Valency is the combining capacity of an atom, that is, its ability to combine with other atoms to form compounds, and it is determined by the number of electrons present in the outermost shell of the atom. Atoms tend to achieve a stable, completely filled outermost shell, and they do so by losing, gaining or sharing electrons; the number of electrons an atom loses, gains or shares to become stable is its valency. If the outermost shell of an atom has only a few electrons, it usually loses them, and its valency equals the number of those electrons, whereas if the outermost shell is nearly full, the atom tends to gain electrons, and its valency equals the number of electrons needed to fill the shell. Atoms with a completely filled outermost shell are stable and have a valency of zero. Valency explains why elements combine in fixed proportions and is essential for writing chemical formulae.

topics.meaning: Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have the same atomic number but different mass numbers due to different numbers of neutrons.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have the same number of protons, and therefore the same atomic number, but different numbers of neutrons, and so different mass numbers. Because isotopes have the same number of protons and electrons, they belong to the same element and have the same chemical properties, but they differ in mass because of the different numbers of neutrons in their nuclei. For example, an element may exist as atoms with the same atomic number but mass numbers that differ by the extra neutrons. Many elements occur naturally as a mixture of isotopes. Isotopes have important uses: some are used in medicine for diagnosis and treatment, and others are used in generating energy or in scientific research. The existence of isotopes shows that atoms of the same element are not always identical in mass, refining the earlier idea from Dalton's theory.

topics.meaning: The law of conservation of mass states that mass is neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The law of conservation of mass states that in a chemical reaction, the total mass of the products is equal to the total mass of the reactants, so mass is neither created nor destroyed during the reaction. This law was proposed based on careful experiments in which substances were reacted in closed containers and weighed before and after the reaction. When the experiment is carried out so that no substance is allowed to escape or enter, the total mass remains the same, showing that the law is obeyed. The reason behind the law is that atoms are simply rearranged during a chemical reaction; no atoms are created or destroyed, so the total mass stays constant. This law is one of the foundational laws of chemical combination and supports the atomic view of matter. It also means that chemical equations must be balanced so that the same number of atoms appears on both sides.

topics.meaning: The law of constant proportions states that a chemical compound always contains the same elements combined in the same fixed proportion by mass.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The law of constant proportions, also called the law of definite proportions, states that a given chemical compound always contains the same elements combined together in the same fixed proportion by mass, no matter how the compound is prepared or where it comes from. For example, water always contains hydrogen and oxygen combined in the same fixed ratio by mass, whether it is obtained from a river, a well, or made in a laboratory. This law was an important foundation for the atomic theory, because the fixed proportions could be explained by atoms of definite mass combining in fixed whole-number ratios. The law allows the composition of a compound to be predicted and checked, and it distinguishes compounds, which have fixed composition, from mixtures, which can have variable composition. Together with the law of conservation of mass, it forms the basis of the laws of chemical combination.

topics.meaning: A covalent bond is formed when two atoms share electrons to achieve a stable, filled outermost shell.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Atoms combine to achieve a stable, completely filled outermost shell, and one way they do this is by sharing electrons. A covalent bond is the bond formed when two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons between them. If two atoms share one pair of electrons, the bond is called a single covalent bond, as in a chlorine molecule where two chlorine atoms each contribute one electron to a shared pair. Atoms can also share two or three pairs of electrons, forming double or triple covalent bonds. When atoms bond by sharing electrons, the substance formed is called a covalent compound. Covalent bonding usually occurs between atoms of non-metals, and the shared electrons are counted as belonging to both atoms, allowing each to attain a stable configuration. Covalent compounds are named by indicating the number of atoms of each element present in the molecule.

topics.meaning: An ionic bond is formed by the transfer of electrons from one atom to another, producing oppositely charged ions that attract each other.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Another way atoms achieve a stable outer shell is by transferring electrons, which forms an ionic bond. In ionic bonding, one atom loses one or more electrons to become a positively charged ion, called a cation, while another atom gains those electrons to become a negatively charged ion, called an anion. For example, a sodium atom loses an electron to form a sodium cation, represented as Na+, while a chlorine atom gains an electron to form a chloride anion. The oppositely charged ions are then held together by a strong electrostatic force of attraction, which constitutes the ionic bond. Substances formed by this transfer of electrons are called ionic compounds. Ionic bonding usually occurs between a metal, which tends to lose electrons, and a non-metal, which tends to gain electrons. Through electron transfer, both atoms attain stable, filled outermost shells.

topics.meaning: A chemical formula uses symbols and valencies to show the kinds and numbers of atoms combined in a compound.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A chemical formula is a way of representing a compound using the symbols of its elements together with numbers showing how many atoms of each are present. To write the formula of a compound, the symbols of the combining elements or ions are written side by side, and their valencies or charges are used to decide the ratio in which they combine. The valency of each element is crossed over to become the subscript of the other, so that the positive and negative combining capacities balance and the compound is electrically neutral. For covalent compounds, the formula shows the number of atoms of each element in a molecule, while for ionic compounds the formula shows the simplest whole-number ratio of the ions. Writing chemical formulae correctly requires knowing the symbols and valencies of the elements and ions involved, and it allows compounds to be represented compactly and consistently.

topics.topic: Properties of Ionic and Covalent Compounds
topics.meaning: Ionic compounds generally have high melting points and conduct electricity when dissolved or molten, while covalent compounds usually have low melting points and do not conduct.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Ionic and covalent compounds differ in their properties because of the different ways their atoms are held together. Ionic compounds are made of oppositely charged ions held by strong electrostatic forces, so they are usually hard solids with high melting and boiling points, and they generally dissolve in water. When dissolved in water or melted, ionic compounds conduct electricity because their ions become free to move and carry charge, but in the solid state the ions are fixed and they do not conduct. Covalent compounds are made of molecules held by weaker forces between molecules, so they usually have low melting and boiling points and may be liquids or gases at room temperature. Most covalent compounds do not conduct electricity because they do not contain free ions or charged particles. These contrasting properties reflect the difference between ionic bonding by electron transfer and covalent bonding by electron sharing.

topics.meaning: The molecular mass of a covalent compound is the sum of the atomic masses of all the atoms present in one molecule of the compound.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The molecular mass of a covalent compound is the sum of the atomic masses of all the atoms present in one molecule of that compound. Since a molecule of a covalent compound is made up of a fixed number of atoms of each element, the molecular mass is found by multiplying the atomic mass of each element by the number of its atoms in the molecule and then adding these values together. The atomic masses of the elements are taken from their relative atomic masses. For example, to find the molecular mass of a compound, one identifies the atoms in its formula, looks up each element's atomic mass, and totals them according to the number of each atom. Molecular mass is a relative quantity expressed in atomic mass units. Knowing how to calculate molecular mass is important for understanding the quantities of substances involved in chemical reactions and for relating mass to the numbers of molecules.

topics.topic: Formula Unit Mass of Ionic Compounds
topics.meaning: The formula unit mass of an ionic compound is the sum of the atomic masses of all the atoms in one formula unit of the compound.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Ionic compounds do not exist as separate molecules but as large arrangements of ions, so instead of a molecular mass we calculate the formula unit mass. The formula unit mass of an ionic compound is the sum of the atomic masses of all the atoms present in one formula unit of the compound, where a formula unit is the simplest whole-number ratio of ions shown by the formula. It is calculated in the same way as molecular mass, by multiplying the atomic mass of each element by the number of its atoms in the formula unit and adding the values together. The atomic masses are taken as relative atomic masses, and the formula unit mass is expressed in atomic mass units. The idea of formula unit mass is needed because ionic compounds are not made of discrete molecules, yet we still need a way to express the mass corresponding to their simplest ratio of ions.

topics.topic: Atoms and Molecules as the Basis of Matter
topics.meaning: Atoms are the basic building blocks of matter that combine to form molecules and compounds, explaining the laws of chemical combination.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The study of how matter is built up from atoms connects the laws of chemical combination with the way atoms join together. Atoms are the basic building blocks of matter, and they combine with one another in fixed whole-number ratios to form molecules and compounds. A molecule is a group of two or more atoms bonded together, and a compound is a substance made of two or more different elements combined in a fixed proportion. The laws of conservation of mass and constant proportions can both be explained by the atomic view: mass is conserved because atoms are only rearranged, and proportions are fixed because atoms of definite mass combine in fixed ratios. Atoms combine either by sharing electrons in covalent bonding or by transferring electrons in ionic bonding, and the resulting compounds have masses that can be calculated from atomic masses. This atomic foundation underlies all of chemistry.
topics.easyExplanation: Atoms are the building blocks of matter; they join in fixed ratios to form molecules and compounds, explaining the chemical laws.

topics.meaning: Sound is produced by vibrating objects, such as a tuning fork, whose back-and-forth motion disturbs the surrounding medium.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Sound is produced by objects that vibrate, that is, by objects moving rapidly back and forth. When an object such as a tuning fork is struck, its prongs vibrate, and these vibrations disturb the air around them. A vibrating object pushes the particles of the surrounding medium when it moves one way and pulls away from them when it moves the other way, repeatedly. This back-and-forth motion of the source creates a disturbance that travels outward as sound. The vibration of a tuning fork can be felt by touching it, and it can be shown by touching the vibrating prong to the surface of water, which splashes due to the vibration. Stringed instruments, drums, and our own vocal cords all produce sound through vibration. When the vibration stops, the production of sound also stops. Thus all sound originates from some vibrating source that sets the surrounding medium into motion.

topics.topic: Sound Needs a Medium to Travel
topics.meaning: Sound requires a material medium such as a solid, liquid or gas to travel, and it cannot travel through a vacuum.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Sound needs a material medium to propagate from one place to another, because it travels by the disturbance of the particles of the medium. As the source vibrates, it sets the nearby particles of the medium into vibration, and these particles pass the disturbance on to the next particles, so the sound travels through the medium without the particles themselves moving from the source to the listener. Because sound depends on particles to carry it, it can travel through solids, liquids and gases, but it cannot travel through a vacuum, which has no particles. This is demonstrated by an experiment in which a ringing electric bell is placed inside a jar; as the air is removed from the jar, the sound becomes fainter and finally cannot be heard, even though the bell is still seen to be working. This confirms that sound cannot travel where there is no medium.
topics.easyExplanation: Sound needs a material like air, water or solids to travel through, and it cannot move through empty space.

topics.meaning: Sound travels as a longitudinal wave, in which the particles of the medium vibrate back and forth along the direction in which the sound travels.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Sound travels through a medium in the form of a wave. As a vibrating object moves forward, it pushes the particles of the medium close together, creating a region of high density and pressure called a compression, and as it moves back it leaves the particles spread apart, creating a region of low density and pressure called a rarefaction. These compressions and rarefactions move forward through the medium one after another, carrying the sound. In this kind of wave the particles of the medium vibrate back and forth along the same direction in which the wave travels, so sound is called a longitudinal wave. The particles themselves do not travel with the wave; they only oscillate about their fixed positions while the disturbance moves forward. The propagation of sound as alternate compressions and rarefactions explains how the vibration of a source is carried through the medium to reach a listener.
topics.easyExplanation: Sound moves as a longitudinal wave of squeezed compressions and spread-out rarefactions, with particles vibrating along the travel direction.

topics.meaning: A sound wave is described by its wavelength, frequency, time period and speed, which together characterise the wave.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A sound wave can be described using several characteristics. The wavelength is the distance between two consecutive compressions or two consecutive rarefactions, that is, the length of one complete wave. The frequency is the number of complete waves, or oscillations, that pass a point in one second, and its unit is the hertz; frequency tells us how many vibrations occur each second. The time period is the time taken for one complete oscillation to pass a point, and it is the reciprocal of the frequency. The speed of the wave is the distance the wave travels in one second, and it is related to the frequency and wavelength by the fact that speed equals frequency multiplied by wavelength. These quantities are connected, so if two of them are known the third can be found. Together, wavelength, frequency, time period and speed completely describe how a sound wave moves through a medium.

topics.meaning: Pitch depends on frequency, loudness depends on amplitude, and quality lets us tell apart sounds of the same pitch and loudness.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Sounds differ in several ways that depend on the characteristics of the sound wave. The pitch of a sound, which is how shrill or how flat it seems, depends on its frequency: a higher frequency produces a higher pitch, while a lower frequency produces a lower pitch. The loudness of a sound depends on the amplitude of the wave, which is the maximum disturbance of the particles from their rest positions; a larger amplitude carries more energy and produces a louder sound, while a smaller amplitude produces a softer sound. The loudness is also related to the intensity of the sound, which is the energy passing per unit area per second. The quality, or timbre, of a sound is what allows us to distinguish between two sounds of the same pitch and loudness, such as the same note played on different instruments. Together, pitch, loudness and quality describe how we perceive different sounds.

topics.meaning: The speed of sound is the distance it travels per unit time, and it depends on the nature and temperature of the medium.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The speed of sound is the distance that a sound wave travels in the medium in one second, and it is related to the frequency and wavelength of the wave. The speed of sound is not the same in all media; it depends on the nature of the medium and on its temperature. Sound generally travels fastest in solids, slower in liquids, and slowest in gases, because the particles in solids are more closely packed and pass the disturbance on more quickly. In a given medium such as air, the speed of sound increases as the temperature rises. The speed of sound in air is much smaller than the speed of light, which is why during a thunderstorm we see the lightning before we hear the thunder, even though both happen at the same time. Knowing the speed of sound is important for understanding echoes and other effects, and for calculating distances using reflected sound.

topics.meaning: Sound reflects off hard surfaces, and a distinct repetition of sound heard after reflection is called an echo.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Sound, like other waves, reflects when it strikes a hard surface, bouncing back into the medium. When a sound is reflected from a distant obstacle and heard again as a distinct, separate repetition of the original sound, it is called an echo. For an echo to be heard clearly, the reflecting surface must be far enough away that the reflected sound returns after a short interval, because the sensation of a sound persists in our ears for a brief time and the echo must arrive after this persistence ends. Using the speed of sound and the time taken for the echo to return, we can calculate the distance of the reflecting surface, since the sound travels to the obstacle and back. Echoes are commonly heard in large empty halls, near cliffs, or in big buildings. The reflection of sound is the basis of many useful applications that rely on detecting reflected sound.

topics.meaning: Reverberation is the persistence of sound due to repeated reflections that overlap, prolonging the sound in an enclosed space.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Reverberation is the persistence of sound in a large enclosed space caused by repeated reflections of the sound from the walls, ceiling and floor. When a sound is produced in such a space, it is reflected many times, and these repeated reflections reach the listener one after another, so the sound seems to continue for a while even after the source has stopped. Some reverberation can make a sound seem fuller, but too much reverberation is undesirable because the overlapping reflections blur the original sound and make speech or music unclear. To reduce excessive reverberation in halls and auditoriums, sound-absorbing materials are used on the walls, ceiling and seats, and curtains and carpets are placed to absorb sound rather than reflect it. The design of large halls therefore takes reverberation into account so that sound is clear. Reverberation is different from an echo, which is a single distinct repetition.

topics.meaning: Echolocation uses reflected sound to detect objects and judge distances, and is used by animals like bats and in technology like sonar.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The reflection of sound is used in echolocation, in which an animal or a device sends out sound and uses the reflected sound, or echo, to detect objects and judge their distance and direction. Some animals, such as bats and dolphins, use echolocation naturally: they emit high-frequency sounds and listen to the echoes that bounce off objects, allowing them to locate prey and navigate even in darkness. The same principle is applied in technology, such as sonar, which sends sound through water and uses the reflected sound to find the distance and location of underwater objects, measure the depth of the sea, and detect things like submarines or shoals of fish. By measuring the time taken for the sound to return and knowing the speed of sound in the medium, the distance to the object can be calculated. These applications show how the reflection of sound is put to practical and useful purposes.

topics.meaning: Asexual reproduction is reproduction involving a single parent that produces offspring genetically identical to the parent, without the fusion of gametes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Asexual reproduction is a mode of reproduction in which a single parent produces new individuals without the involvement of gametes or the fusion of sex cells. Because only one parent is involved and no mixing of genetic material occurs, the offspring produced are genetically identical to the parent. There are several methods of asexual reproduction in different organisms, such as the formation of new individuals from buds, the breaking of the body into parts that each grow into a new organism, and the development of plantlets on leaves. Asexual reproduction is generally faster and allows organisms to multiply quickly when conditions are favourable, since it does not require finding a partner. However, because the offspring are identical to the parent, asexual reproduction does not create genetic variation among the offspring. This makes it useful for rapid increase in number but less able to adapt to changing environments compared with sexual reproduction.

topics.meaning: Vegetative propagation is asexual reproduction in plants from vegetative parts such as roots, stems and leaves.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Vegetative propagation is a form of asexual reproduction in plants in which new plants are produced from the vegetative parts of a plant, such as roots, stems and leaves, rather than from seeds. In this process, parts of the parent plant develop into complete new plants that are genetically identical to the parent. Many plants reproduce this way naturally, and people also use vegetative propagation deliberately to grow new plants. This method is helpful because it allows plants to be reproduced quickly, the new plants are identical to the parent so desirable features are retained, and plants that do not produce viable seeds can still be multiplied. It also allows farmers and gardeners to grow many plants in a short time and to preserve particular varieties. Vegetative propagation is widely used in agriculture and horticulture to produce large numbers of plants with the same useful characteristics as the parent plant.

topics.meaning: Sexual reproduction involves two parents and the fusion of gametes, producing offspring with genetic variation through processes including meiosis.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Sexual reproduction is a mode of reproduction that involves two parents and the fusion of two gametes, one from each parent, to form a new individual. Unlike asexual reproduction, it mixes the genetic material of two parents, so the offspring are not identical to either parent and show genetic variation. The gametes are produced by a special type of cell division called meiosis, which creates variation by shuffling and reducing the genetic material so that each gamete carries a different combination, and by ensuring the offspring receives a mix of genes from both parents. This genetic variation is important because it gives a population a better chance of surviving changes in the environment, since some individuals may have combinations of features that help them cope with new conditions. Sexual reproduction is therefore slower and requires two parents, but it is a major source of variation that supports the long-term survival and evolution of species.

topics.meaning: In flowering plants, sexual reproduction occurs in the flower, which contains the male and female reproductive parts.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In flowering plants, the flower is the reproductive structure where sexual reproduction takes place, and it contains the male and female reproductive parts. The male part produces pollen grains, which contain the male gametes, while the female part contains the ovules, which hold the female gametes. For reproduction to occur, pollen must be transferred from the male part to the female part, after which the male and female gametes can come together. The flower is often supported by structures that protect the developing parts and, in many plants, attract agents that help transfer pollen. After the gametes fuse, the ovule develops into a seed and the surrounding part of the flower develops into a fruit. The flower thus brings together the structures needed for the male and female gametes to meet, making sexual reproduction possible in flowering plants and leading to the formation of seeds that can grow into new plants.

topics.meaning: Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the male part of a flower to the female part, and it may occur by agents such as wind, water and insects.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Pollination is the process of transfer of pollen grains from the male part of a flower to the female part, which is a necessary step before fertilisation can occur. Pollination can take place in different ways, depending on the agent that carries the pollen. Some plants are pollinated with the help of wind, which carries light pollen from one flower to another, while others rely on water. Many plants are pollinated by insects and other animals, which visit flowers and carry pollen on their bodies from one flower to another; such flowers are often coloured, scented, or produce nectar to attract these pollinators. These different pollination strategies improve the chances of successful reproduction by ensuring that pollen reaches the female part of a flower. The transfer of pollen by various agents is important because it brings the male gametes close to the female gametes, allowing fertilisation and the formation of seeds to follow.

topics.meaning: Fertilisation is the fusion of male and female gametes to form a zygote, after which the ovule develops into a seed and the ovary into a fruit.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Fertilisation is the process in which the male gamete fuses with the female gamete to form a single cell called the zygote, which marks the beginning of a new individual. In flowering plants, after pollination the male gamete reaches the female gamete inside the ovule, and their fusion completes fertilisation. Following fertilisation, the zygote develops into an embryo, the ovule develops into a seed that contains the embryo and stored food, and the surrounding ovary develops into a fruit that protects the seed. The seed can later germinate under suitable conditions to grow into a new plant. Fertilisation is a crucial step because it combines the genetic material of the two gametes, producing offspring with a new combination of features. Thus the sequence of pollination followed by fertilisation leads to the formation of seeds and fruits, ensuring that flowering plants can produce the next generation.

topics.meaning: Reproductive maturity is the stage at which the body becomes capable of reproduction, marked by physical and other changes at puberty.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: As humans grow, the body undergoes changes that prepare it for reproduction, and the stage at which a person becomes capable of reproduction is called reproductive maturity. The period during which these changes occur is called puberty, when the reproductive organs mature and the body shows a number of physical changes. During puberty, there are visible changes in the body of both males and females, along with the maturing of the reproductive system so that gametes begin to be produced. These changes are part of normal growth and development, and they happen gradually over a period of time rather than all at once. Reaching reproductive maturity means that the body is physically capable of producing gametes and, in principle, of reproduction. Understanding these changes is important for understanding how the human body develops, and being sexually mature involves not only physical development but also emotional and social readiness for the responsibilities that reproduction involves.

topics.meaning: The human male and female reproductive systems contain organs that produce gametes and support fertilisation and the development of a new individual.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Human beings reproduce sexually, and they have separate male and female reproductive systems made up of organs that perform specific roles. The male reproductive system produces the male gametes and includes the parts that make and deliver them. The female reproductive system produces the female gametes and includes the structures where fertilisation occurs and where a new individual can develop, such as the uterus. In reproduction, the male gamete and the female gamete are brought together, and when a male gamete meets and fuses with a female gamete, fertilisation takes place, forming a zygote. The female reproductive system is also where the fertilised egg develops during pregnancy. Each part of the male and female reproductive systems has a particular function, and together they make sexual reproduction possible in humans by producing gametes, allowing them to meet, and providing a place for the new individual to grow.

topics.meaning: Pregnancy is the period during which a fertilised egg develops into a baby inside the mother's uterus, ending in childbirth.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When a sperm fertilises an egg, the resulting zygote begins to divide and develops inside the mother's uterus, beginning pregnancy. During pregnancy, the developing individual grows inside the uterus over a period of several months, receiving nourishment from the mother, and the mother's body supports this development. Because the developing baby depends entirely on the mother, the mother's health during pregnancy is very important, and she needs proper nutrition and care so that the baby develops well. At the end of pregnancy, childbirth takes place, when the fully developed baby is born. If a released egg is not fertilised, it does not develop into a new individual, and the body removes it along with the thickened inner lining prepared in the uterus. Understanding pregnancy and childbirth helps explain how a new human individual develops from a single fertilised cell into a baby and the importance of caring for the mother during this time.

topics.meaning: Reproductive health means being physically, mentally and socially healthy in matters relating to reproduction, including preventing unwanted pregnancies and infections.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Reproductive health refers to being healthy in all matters relating to the reproductive system, including physical, mental and social well-being. As people reach reproductive maturity, it becomes important to understand how to stay healthy and make responsible decisions. Unwanted pregnancies and infections that can spread through reproductive contact are concerns that can be prevented through awareness and appropriate measures. Maintaining cleanliness and good hygiene of the reproductive organs is an important part of reproductive health, and being informed helps people protect themselves and make responsible choices. Some infections can be passed from one person to another and can be avoided by taking suitable precautions. Reproductive health also includes understanding that being sexually mature involves emotional and social readiness, not just physical development. Being well informed about reproductive health enables individuals to take care of their bodies, prevent problems, and make sensible and responsible decisions as they grow into adulthood.

chapters.chapter: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

topics.meaning: Biodiversity is the variety of living organisms, and India is rich in biodiversity, qualifying as a biodiversity hotspot.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Biodiversity refers to the variety of living organisms found in an area, including the different kinds of plants, animals and microorganisms. India is extraordinarily rich in biodiversity because of its varied climate and geography, ranging from mountains to coasts to forests, and it is considered a biodiversity hotspot, an area with a great variety of species. This richness includes many species that are found in India and a number that are found nowhere else. The wide range of habitats in the country supports a large number of different organisms, making India one of the most biologically diverse regions in the world. Recognising and studying this biodiversity is important because it represents a vast natural heritage and because the variety of life supports the functioning of ecosystems. The great diversity of organisms also raises the question of how such variety arose and how we can make sense of it through classification.

topics.meaning: The great diversity of life on Earth has arisen over a very long time through gradual change in organisms.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The enormous variety of organisms seen today did not appear all at once but has developed over an extremely long period of time. Life on Earth began with simple forms, and over millions of years organisms gradually changed, giving rise to the wide range of living things present now. These changes happened slowly across many generations, and new kinds of organisms arose from earlier ones. The study of how life has changed over time shows that present-day organisms are connected to those that lived in the past, and that the diversity of life is the result of this long history of change. Evidence such as the remains of organisms preserved from long ago helps us understand this gradual development. Recognising that biodiversity has evolved over time helps explain why there are so many different kinds of organisms and why they show both differences and similarities, which becomes the basis for classifying them.

topics.meaning: Classification is the grouping of organisms based on similarities and differences, which makes the study of the vast diversity of life manageable.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Because there is an enormous variety of living organisms, studying each one separately would be impossible, so scientists group organisms in a systematic way, which is called classification. Classification means arranging organisms into groups based on their similarities and differences, so that organisms sharing common features are placed together. This makes it much easier to study, identify and understand the relationships among living things, because once an organism is placed in a group, we know it shares the features of that group. Classification also helps reveal how different organisms are related to one another and provides an orderly framework for organising knowledge about life. Some criteria used to classify organisms include whether they are made of one cell or many, how they obtain their food, and other features of their structure and functioning. By grouping organisms according to such criteria, classification turns the overwhelming diversity of life into an organised and understandable system.

topics.meaning: The five kingdom classification divides all living organisms into Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Classification systems have developed over time, and a widely used system divides all living organisms into five large groups called kingdoms. These five kingdoms are Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia. Organisms are placed into these kingdoms based on features such as whether their cells are prokaryotic or eukaryotic, whether they are unicellular or multicellular, and how they obtain their food. The five kingdom system improved on earlier systems that divided life into only plants and animals, because it could account for organisms such as bacteria and fungi that did not fit neatly into either. By using a few broad and important features, the five kingdom classification provides an orderly way to group the great diversity of organisms. Each kingdom contains organisms that share certain fundamental characteristics, and within each kingdom organisms are further divided into smaller groups, giving a hierarchical structure to the classification of life.

topics.meaning: Monera consists of unicellular prokaryotes such as bacteria, while Protista consists of unicellular eukaryotes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two of the five kingdoms contain mostly single-celled organisms but differ in the kind of cell they have. The kingdom Monera consists of unicellular prokaryotes, that is, single-celled organisms whose cells lack a true nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; bacteria are the well-known members of this kingdom. The kingdom Protista consists of unicellular eukaryotes, that is, single-celled organisms whose cells have a true nucleus and membrane-bound organelles. The key difference between the two kingdoms is therefore the type of cell: Monera have prokaryotic cells, while Protista have eukaryotic cells. Members of these kingdoms obtain food in various ways, some making their own food and others taking it in. Separating these single-celled organisms into two kingdoms reflects the fundamental difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, which is one of the most important distinctions in the living world and a major basis for classification.

topics.meaning: Fungi are multicellular heterotrophs, Plantae are multicellular autotrophs, and Animalia are multicellular heterotrophs.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Three of the five kingdoms contain mainly multicellular organisms that differ in how they obtain their food. The kingdom Fungi consists of organisms that are heterotrophic, meaning they obtain food from other sources rather than making it themselves, often absorbing nutrients from their surroundings. The kingdom Plantae consists of multicellular organisms that are autotrophic, meaning they make their own food using sunlight through the green pigment in their cells, and they have cell walls. The kingdom Animalia consists of multicellular organisms that are heterotrophic, obtaining food by taking in other organisms, and they do not have cell walls. The main features distinguishing these kingdoms are whether the organisms make their own food or obtain it from elsewhere, and the presence or absence of a cell wall. Grouping organisms in this way reflects fundamental differences in structure and nutrition and helps organise the great variety of multicellular life.

topics.meaning: Classification is hierarchical, with organisms grouped into progressively smaller and more specific levels from kingdom down to species.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Classification is organised in a hierarchy, meaning that organisms are grouped into a series of levels, with each level being more specific than the one above it. The largest and broadest groups are the kingdoms, and within each kingdom organisms are divided into smaller groups, which are themselves divided into still smaller groups, continuing down to the most specific level. At each step down the hierarchy, the organisms in a group share more features in common and are more similar to one another, while at higher levels the groups are larger and the organisms share only a few broad features. The smallest and most specific group in this hierarchy is the species, which contains organisms that are very similar to one another. This hierarchical arrangement reflects the relationships among organisms, since organisms placed close together in the hierarchy are more closely related. The hierarchy makes the classification of life orderly and shows how broad and specific groupings fit together.

topics.meaning: The binomial system gives each species a two-part scientific name so that organisms can be identified consistently worldwide.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Common names of organisms differ from place to place and from language to language, which can cause confusion, so scientists use a standard system of naming called the binomial system. In this system, each species is given a scientific name made up of two parts, the first part indicating the larger group to which the organism belongs and the second part identifying the particular species. These scientific names are recognised and used by scientists all over the world, regardless of language, so that everyone refers to the same organism by the same name. The scientific name is written in a particular standard way to make it easy to recognise. Using a single agreed name for each species avoids the confusion caused by many different common names and allows scientists everywhere to communicate clearly about organisms. The binomial system is therefore an essential tool for the precise identification and study of the great diversity of living things.

topics.meaning: Fossils are the preserved remains or impressions of organisms that lived long ago, providing evidence of how life has changed over time.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Fossils are the preserved remains, impressions or traces of organisms that lived in the distant past, often found in rocks. They form when the remains of organisms are buried and preserved over very long periods of time. Fossils are important because they provide direct evidence that many kinds of organisms lived on Earth long ago, including some that no longer exist today. By studying fossils, scientists can learn about organisms of the past and see how living things have changed over time, since older rocks contain fossils of simpler organisms while more recent rocks contain fossils more similar to present-day organisms. Fossils therefore help show that life on Earth has a long history and that present organisms are connected to those of the past. This evidence supports the idea that biodiversity has developed gradually over time, and it helps scientists understand the relationships among different groups of organisms in classification.

topics.meaning: Biodiversity is under threat from human activities, and conserving the variety of life is important for the health of ecosystems and people.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Although the Earth is rich in biodiversity, this variety of life is under threat, largely because of human activities. Activities such as the destruction of natural habitats, pollution, and the overuse of natural resources reduce the number and variety of organisms and can drive some species towards disappearing altogether. The loss of biodiversity is a serious concern because the variety of organisms supports the functioning of ecosystems on which all living things, including humans, depend, and once a kind of organism is lost it cannot be brought back. Conserving biodiversity means protecting habitats, using resources wisely, and taking care not to harm the variety of life. Understanding the value of biodiversity and the threats it faces encourages responsible behaviour towards the natural world. Protecting biodiversity is important not only for the organisms themselves but also for maintaining the balance of nature and the well-being of present and future generations.

chapters.chapter: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

topics.meaning: The Earth is heated unevenly by the Sun, mainly because of its curved shape, which causes different regions to receive different amounts of solar energy.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Sun is the main source of energy for the Earth, but the Earth is not heated evenly everywhere. The most important reason is the curved, roughly spherical shape of the Earth, which causes sunlight to fall more directly near the equator and more slantingly near the poles. Where sunlight falls more directly, the same amount of solar energy is concentrated on a smaller area, so those regions, such as near the equator, are heated more strongly. Where sunlight falls at a slant, near the poles, the same energy is spread over a larger area, so those regions are heated less and remain colder. This uneven heating means different parts of the Earth receive different amounts of solar energy and reach different temperatures. The uneven heating of the Earth's surface is the starting point for understanding many large-scale processes, including the movement of air and water across the planet.

topics.topic: Role of the Atmosphere in Heating
topics.meaning: The atmosphere influences how the Earth is heated by absorbing, reflecting and trapping solar energy, helping regulate the planet's temperature.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The atmosphere is the layer of gases surrounding the Earth, and it plays an important role in how the planet is heated. When solar radiation reaches the Earth, some of it is reflected back, some is absorbed by the atmosphere, and some reaches the surface and warms it. The atmosphere does not let all the heat escape back into space; certain gases trap some of the heat, keeping the Earth warm enough to support life. Without the atmosphere, the Earth's surface would experience far greater extremes of temperature between day and night. The atmosphere therefore acts somewhat like a blanket, moderating the temperature of the Earth. The way the atmosphere absorbs, reflects and traps solar energy, together with the uneven heating of the surface, affects the temperature of different regions and influences the movement of air. Understanding the role of the atmosphere helps explain how the Earth maintains conditions suitable for living things.

topics.meaning: Winds are the movement of air caused by the uneven heating of the Earth, which creates differences in air pressure.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Winds are the movement of air from one place to another, and they are caused by the uneven heating of the Earth's surface. When air is heated, it becomes lighter and rises, leaving behind a region of low pressure, while cooler, heavier air sinks and creates a region of higher pressure. Air then moves from regions of higher pressure to regions of lower pressure, and this moving air is what we call wind. Because the Earth is heated unevenly, different regions develop different pressures, setting the air in motion. Winds that blow over small areas due to local heating are called local winds, while winds that blow over very large parts of the Earth in regular patterns are called planetary winds. These large-scale wind patterns are part of the way the Earth redistributes heat from warmer regions to cooler ones. Winds are an important part of how energy and matter move through the Earth system.

topics.meaning: Ocean currents are large-scale movements of seawater that, like winds, are driven partly by uneven heating and help redistribute heat around the Earth.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Ocean currents are the continuous, large-scale movements of seawater in the oceans, flowing along definite paths. Like winds in the atmosphere, ocean currents are connected to the uneven heating of the Earth, and they are influenced by winds blowing over the ocean surface and by differences in the temperature of the water. Warm ocean currents carry warm water from warmer regions towards cooler regions, while cold currents carry cooler water in the opposite direction. In this way, ocean currents move heat from one part of the Earth to another, helping to redistribute energy across the planet, much as winds do in the atmosphere. The movement of water in ocean currents also affects the climate of nearby coastal regions, making them warmer or cooler than they would otherwise be. Ocean currents are therefore an important part of how the Earth system moves energy and matter around the globe.

topics.meaning: Biogeochemical cycles are the pathways by which matter such as water, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen moves through the living and non-living parts of the Earth.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Matter on the Earth is not used up but is repeatedly recycled through the environment, moving between the living organisms and the non-living surroundings such as the air, water, soil and rocks. The pathways by which important substances move through these living and non-living parts of the Earth are called biogeochemical cycles, because they involve biological, geological and chemical processes. The main biogeochemical cycles include the water cycle, the carbon cycle, the nitrogen cycle and the oxygen cycle. In each cycle, a substance passes from the environment into living organisms and back again, so the same matter is used over and over. These cycles connect the different parts of the Earth system and ensure a continuous supply of the substances that organisms need. The recycling of matter through biogeochemical cycles, powered ultimately by energy from the Sun, is essential for sustaining life on Earth and maintaining the balance of the environment.

topics.quickSummary: Biogeochemical cycles are the pathways by which matter moves through the living and non-living parts of the Earth, involving biological, geological and chemical processes. The main cycles are the water, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen cycles. In each, a substance passes from the environment into organisms and back, so matter is recycled rather than used up. Powered by the Sun, these cycles connect the Earth system and sustain life.

topics.meaning: The water cycle is the continuous movement of water through evaporation, condensation and precipitation between the Earth's surface and the atmosphere.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The water cycle is the continuous circulation of water between the Earth's surface and the atmosphere, driven by energy from the Sun. In this cycle, water from oceans, rivers, lakes and the land is heated by the Sun and changes into water vapour by the process of evaporation, rising into the atmosphere. As the water vapour rises and cools, it condenses to form tiny droplets that make clouds, a process called condensation. When these droplets combine and become heavy enough, they fall back to the Earth as precipitation in the form of rain or snow. The water that falls flows over the land into rivers and oceans or soaks into the ground, and the cycle repeats. The water cycle continuously moves and recycles water through the environment, ensuring a supply of fresh water for living things and connecting the atmosphere, the oceans, the land and living organisms in the Earth system.
topics.easyExplanation: The water cycle moves water round and round through evaporation, cloud-forming condensation and rain falling back to Earth.

topics.meaning: The carbon cycle is the movement of carbon between the atmosphere, living organisms, the oceans and the land through processes such as photosynthesis and respiration.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The carbon cycle is the continuous movement of carbon through the living and non-living parts of the Earth. Carbon is present in the atmosphere as carbon dioxide, and plants take in this carbon dioxide and use it to make food through photosynthesis, so carbon becomes part of the bodies of living organisms. When organisms respire, they release carbon dioxide back into the atmosphere, and carbon also returns to the environment when organisms die and decompose. Burning of fuels also releases carbon dioxide into the air. The oceans too absorb and release carbon dioxide, taking part in the cycle. Through these processes, carbon passes from the atmosphere into living things and back again, so the same carbon is used over and over. The carbon cycle connects plants, animals, the atmosphere and the oceans, and it is essential for life because carbon is a key element in all living things and in the food they depend on.

topics.meaning: The nitrogen cycle and the oxygen cycle move nitrogen and oxygen between the atmosphere, living organisms and the environment.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two more important biogeochemical cycles are the nitrogen cycle and the oxygen cycle. Nitrogen is the most abundant gas in the atmosphere, but most living organisms cannot use it directly from the air. In the nitrogen cycle, nitrogen from the air is converted into forms that plants can take up and use to make important substances, these substances pass to animals that eat the plants, and nitrogen is later returned to the soil and the air when organisms die and decompose. The oxygen cycle involves the movement of oxygen through the environment: organisms use oxygen from the air during respiration and release carbon dioxide, while plants release oxygen into the air during photosynthesis, replenishing it. Through these cycles, nitrogen and oxygen are continuously recycled between the atmosphere, living organisms and the environment. Both cycles are essential for life, since nitrogen and oxygen are needed by living things, and they show how matter is shared and reused across the Earth system.

topics.topic: The Earth as an Interconnected System
topics.meaning: The Earth functions as a single system in which the atmosphere, oceans, land and living organisms are connected through flows of energy and matter.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Earth can be understood as a single, interconnected system in which the air, the water, the land and living organisms all interact with one another. Energy from the Sun and matter such as water, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen flow between these different parts, linking them together. The uneven heating of the Earth sets air and water in motion as winds and ocean currents, which carry energy from one place to another, while biogeochemical cycles move matter between the living and non-living parts. Because the parts are connected, a change in one part of the system can affect the others; for example, changes in the atmosphere can affect the climate and living things. Viewing the Earth as a system helps us see how energy, matter and life are tied together and why processes that seem separate are actually parts of one larger whole. This systems view is important for understanding the natural world and the effects of human activities.

topics.meaning: Human activities can change the Earth's natural processes, affecting the atmosphere, the climate and the cycles of matter, so responsible action is needed.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Human activities have become powerful enough to change the Earth's natural processes and disturb the balance of the Earth system. Activities such as burning large amounts of fuel, clearing forests, and producing waste add substances to the atmosphere and the environment and interfere with the natural cycles of matter and the flow of energy. For example, adding more carbon dioxide to the atmosphere can affect the way the atmosphere traps heat and can influence the climate. Because the parts of the Earth system are connected, such changes can spread and affect the air, water, land and living organisms. The effects of human activities on the Earth's processes are a serious concern, since they can disturb the conditions that support life. Understanding how the Earth works as a system helps us recognise the consequences of our actions and the importance of acting responsibly, using resources carefully and reducing harm so that the balance of the Earth system is maintained.
